title: EXPOSÉ VII B INFINITESIMAL STUDY OF GROUP SCHEMES

pages.title: EXPOSÉ VII B INFINITESIMAL STUDY OF GROUP SCHEMES

blocks.html:
EXPOSÉ VII B
INFINITESIMAL STUDY OF GROUP SCHEMESby P. Gabriel
B) Formal groups

The study of formal groups is usually of an extreme simplicity. If this does not appear clearly in the pages that follow, the responsibility falls on an arithmetician, who claims to know formal groups over "something other than fields". We have therefore unrolled, for formal groups "locally free over projective limits of artinian rings", the generalities that are usually stated for formal groups defined over a field. For a more detailed study of the latter, we refer to the 1964/65 Heidelberg-Strasbourg seminar on algebraic geometry.

pages.title: 0. Reminders on pseudocompact rings and modules

blocks.html: 0. Reminders on pseudocompact rings and modules
This section contains a few technical preliminaries; in it we recall and complete some results of C.A. (Des catégories abéliennes, Bul. Soc. math. de France, 90, 1962).

A left pseudocompact ring is a topological ring with identity element, separated, complete, and possessing a base of neighborhoods of 0 formed of left ideals \(\mathfrak{l}\) of finite colength (i.e. \(\mathrm{long}_A(A/\mathfrak{l}) < +\infty\)). We shall assume here that A is commutative, so that there is no need to distinguish "between left and right". In particular, the quotients \(A/\mathfrak{l}\) are equipped with structures of artinian ring and A is identified with the topological projective limit of these rings equipped with the discrete topology.
A complete noetherian local ring is obviously pseudocompact.

A closed ideal of A is the intersection of the open ideals containing it. A maximal closed ideal is therefore open. Moreover, if \(\mathfrak{l}\) is an open ideal of A, the maximal ideals of \(A/\mathfrak{l}\) correspond bijectively to the closed maximal ideals \(\mathfrak{m}\) containing \(\mathfrak{l}\). The localization \((A/\mathfrak{l})_{\mathfrak{m}}\) of \(A/\mathfrak{l}\) with respect to a closed maximal ideal \(\mathfrak{m}\) is therefore a local ring if \(\mathfrak{m}\) contains \(\mathfrak{l}\) and is zero otherwise. As the ring \(A/\mathfrak{l}\) is artinian, it is a direct product of a finite number of local rings, which can be written \[ A/\mathfrak{l} \simeq \prod_{\mathfrak{m}\in\Upsilon(A)} (A/\mathfrak{l})_{\mathfrak{m}} \] where \(\Upsilon(A)\) denotes the set of closed maximal ideals of A. From this we obtain "canonical" isomorphisms \[ A \simeq \varprojlim_{\mathfrak{l}} (A/\mathfrak{l}) \simeq \varprojlim_{\mathfrak{l}} \prod_{\mathfrak{m}} (A/\mathfrak{l})_{\mathfrak{m}} \simeq \prod_{\mathfrak{m}} \varprojlim_{\mathfrak{l}} (A/\mathfrak{l})_{\mathfrak{m}}, \] where we have set \(A_{\mathfrak{m}} = \varprojlim_{\mathfrak{l}} (A/\mathfrak{l})_{\mathfrak{m}}\). This local component \(A_{\mathfrak{m}}\) is a filtered projective limit of local rings; it is therefore a local ring which is pseudocompact for the projective limit topology (the rings \((A/\mathfrak{l})_{\mathfrak{m}}\) are artinian and equipped with the discrete topology).

Let \(\mathfrak{r}(A)\) be the intersection of the closed maximal ideals of A, that is to say the cartesian product of the ideals \(\mathfrak{m}A_{\mathfrak{m}}\) when A is identified with \(\prod_{\mathfrak{m}} A_{\mathfrak{m}}\). For every open ideal \(\mathfrak{l}\) of A, the image of \(\mathfrak{r}(A)\) in \(A/\mathfrak{l}\) is contained in the radical of \(A/\mathfrak{l}\). Some power of this image is therefore zero, so that \(\mathfrak{r}(A)^n\) is contained in \(\mathfrak{l}\) when n is large enough. The sequence of the \(\mathfrak{r}(A)^n\) therefore tends to 0; the same holds for the sequence of the \(x^n\), when x belongs to \(\mathfrak{r}(A)\). In other words, every element of \(\mathfrak{r}(A)\) is topologically nilpotent, and the converse is clear. It follows that the sequence with general term \(1+x+\cdots+x^n\) is convergent and converges to \(1/(1-x)\) when \(x\in\mathfrak{r}(A)\). This shows that \(\mathfrak{r}(A)\) is the Jacobson radical of A (Bourb., Alg., chap. 8, § 6, th. 1).

If A and B are two pseudocompact rings, a homomorphism from A to B is, by definition, a continuous map compatible with addition, multiplication and the identity elements. Such a homomorphism sends a topologically nilpotent element of A to a topologically nilpotent element of B; it therefore maps the radical of A into the radical of B.

Let A be a (commutative) pseudocompact ring. A pseudocompact A-module M is a topological A-module, separated, complete, unitary, which possesses a base of neighborhoods of 0 formed of submodules M′ such that M/M′ is of finite length over A. If M and N are two pseudocompact A-modules, a morphism from M to N is by definition a continuous A-linear map. The pseudocompact A-modules form an abelian category whose cogenerators are the pseudocompact A-modules of finite length (whose topology is therefore discrete). If \((M_i)\) is a projective system of pseudocompact modules, the projective limit of the \(M_i\) has for underlying module the projective limit of the underlying modules, and for topology that of the projective limit. Moreover, the projective limit functor is exact when the indices form a filtered ordered set (Axiom \(\mathrm{AB5}^*\)). Likewise, if f is a morphism of pseudocompact A-modules, \(\mathrm{Coker}\,f\) has for underlying module the cokernel of the underlying modules and the topology of \(\mathrm{Coker}\,f\) is the quotient of that of the target of f (confer [C.A.], IV, § 3).

Each local component \(A_{\mathfrak{m}}\) of A is a direct factor of A, hence a projective object of the category of pseudocompact A-modules (A is manifestly projective). It follows from C.A. (IV, § 3, cor. 1 to theo. 3) that every projective pseudocompact A-module is a direct (topological) product of such \(A_{\mathfrak{m}}\).

A pseudocompact A-module M is said to be topologically free if it is isomorphic to the product of a family \((A_i)\) of copies of A. A family \((m_i)\) of elements of M is called a pseudobasis of M if the A-linear maps from \(A_i\) to M sending the identity element of A to \(m_i\) extend to an isomorphism of \(\prod_i A_i\) onto M.

If M is a pseudocompact A-module, \(M^*\) denotes the A-module \(\mathrm{Hom}_c(M,A)\) formed of the continuous A-linear maps from M to A. When the ring A is artinian, the functor \(P \mapsto P^*\) is an anti-equivalence from the category of projective pseudocompact A-modules to that of projective A-modules. In particular, when A is a field, \(P \mapsto P^*\) is an antiequivalence from the category of all pseudocompact A-modules (one also speaks of linearly compact vector spaces over A) to that of A-vector spaces.

Let M and N be two pseudocompact A-modules and \(\mathrm{Hom}_c(M,N)\) the group of morphisms from M to N. Denote moreover by \(\mathbf{h}_c^M\) the functor which associates to every pseudocompact A-module of finite length N the abelian group \(\mathrm{Hom}_c(M,N)\). It is then known that the functor \(M \mapsto \mathbf{h}_c^M\) is an antiequivalence from the category of pseudocompact A-modules to that of left exact functors (C.A., II, theo. 1). If L and M are two pseudocompact A-modules, one therefore defines, up to isomorphism, a pseudocompact A-module \(L \widehat{\otimes}_A M\) by requiring that the functor \(N \mapsto \mathrm{Hom}_c(L \widehat{\otimes}_A M, N)\) be isomorphic to the functor \(N \mapsto \mathrm{Bil}_c(L\times M, N)\), where \(\mathrm{Bil}_c(L\times M,N)\) denotes the set of continuous A-bilinear maps from \(L\times M\) into a module of finite length N.

One can describe \(L \widehat{\otimes}_A M\) as the projective limit of the (discrete) A-modules \((L/L')\otimes_A(M/M')\), where \(L'\) and \(M'\) run respectively over the open submodules of L and of M: indeed, let \(\varphi : L\times M \to N\) be a continuous bilinear map from \(L\times M\) into a (discrete) A-module of finite length N. By lemma 0.3.1 below, there exist open submodules \(L'\) and \(M'\) of L and M such that \(\varphi(L'\times M) = \varphi(L\times M') = \{0\}\). This means that the map \(\overline{\varphi} : L\otimes_A M \to N\), induced by \(\varphi\), is of the form \(\varphi' \circ p\), where p is the canonical projection of \(L\otimes_A M\) onto \((L/L')\otimes_A(M/M')\). If \(\widehat{\varphi}\) is obtained by composing \(\varphi'\) with the canonical projection of the projective limit \(L \widehat{\otimes}_A M\) onto \((L/L')\otimes_A(M/M')\), it is clear that the map \(\varphi \mapsto \widehat{\varphi}\) is a bijection from \(\mathrm{Bil}_c(L\times M,N)\) onto \(\mathrm{Hom}_c(L \widehat{\otimes}_A M, N)\).

The pseudocompact module \(L \widehat{\otimes}_A M\) is therefore the separated completion of \(L\otimes_A M\) for the linear topology defined by the kernels of the canonical projections of \(L\otimes_A M\) onto \((L/L')\otimes_A(M/M')\). If x and y belong to L and M, the image of \(x\otimes_A y\) in the completed tensor product \(L \widehat{\otimes}_A M\) will be denoted \(x \widehat{\otimes}_A y\).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 0.3.1

Let L, M and N be pseudocompact A-modules, N being of finite length. If \(\varphi : L\times M \to N\) is a continuous A-bilinear map, there exist open submodules \(L'\) and \(M'\) of L and M such that \(\varphi(L'\times M) = \varphi(L\times M') = \{0\}\).

Indeed, \(\varphi^{-1}(0)\) is an open neighborhood of \((0,0)\), hence contains an open set of the form \(L'\times M'\), where \(L'\) and \(M'\) are open submodules of L and M. As \(L/L'\) is of finite length, there exist elements \(x_1,\ldots,x_r\) of L such that \(L'+Ax_1+\cdots+Ax_r = L\). If \(M'\) is "small enough", we also have \(\varphi(x_i,M') = 0\) for every i, because the map \(y \mapsto (x_i,y)\) is continuous; from this we obtain \(\varphi(L,M') = \{0\}\); likewise, \(\varphi(L',M) = \{0\}\) if \(L'\) is small enough.

Let \(L' \xrightarrow{f} L \xrightarrow{g} L'' \to 0\) be an exact sequence of pseudocompact A-modules. It is clear that the induced sequence \[ 0 \longrightarrow \mathrm{Bil}_c(L''\times M,N) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Bil}_c(L\times M,N) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Bil}_c(L'\times M,N) \] is exact for every pseudocompact A-module M and every N of finite length. This means that the sequence of left exact functors represented by \(L'' \widehat{\otimes}_A M\), \(L \widehat{\otimes}_A M\) and \(L' \widehat{\otimes}_A M\) is exact, in other words that the sequence \[ (*)\qquad L' \widehat{\otimes}_A M \xrightarrow{f\widehat{\otimes}M} L \widehat{\otimes}_A M \xrightarrow{g\widehat{\otimes}M} L'' \widehat{\otimes}_A M \longrightarrow 0 \] is exact: the completed tensor product functor is right exact.

Take in particular for L the ring A, for f the inclusion of a closed ideal \(\mathfrak{a}\) into A, for g the canonical projection of A onto \(A/\mathfrak{a}\). One can then identify \(A \widehat{\otimes}_A M\) with M by means of the map \(x \widehat{\otimes} y \mapsto x\cdot y\). As the image of \(\mathfrak{a} \widehat{\otimes}_A M\) is closed in M (C.A., IV, § 3) and the image of \(\mathfrak{a}\otimes_A M\) is everywhere dense in \(\mathfrak{a} \widehat{\otimes}_A M\), the image of \(f\widehat{\otimes}M\) is none other than the closure \(\overline{\mathfrak{a}\cdot M}\) of the product \(\mathfrak{a}\cdot M\) in M. The sequence \((*)\) therefore implies that \((A/\mathfrak{a}) \widehat{\otimes}_A M\) is identified with \(M/\overline{\mathfrak{a}\cdot M}\).

blocks.title: Nakayama's Lemma
blocks.html:
Nakayama's Lemma 0.3.3

Let A be a pseudocompact ring, M a pseudocompact A-module and \(\mathfrak{a}\) an ideal of A contained in the radical \(\mathfrak{r}(A)\). Then the equality \(\overline{\mathfrak{a}\cdot M} = M\) implies \(M = 0\).

Indeed, let \(M'\) be an open submodule of M and \(M''\) the quotient \(M/M'\). As \(M''\) is discrete, \(\mathfrak{a}\cdot M''\) is closed in \(M''\), hence equal to \(\overline{\mathfrak{a}\cdot M''}\). By 0.3.2, the canonical map from \(M/\overline{\mathfrak{a}\cdot M}\) to \(M''/\overline{\mathfrak{a}\cdot M''}\) is surjective, so that we have \(\mathfrak{a}\cdot M'' = \overline{\mathfrak{a}\cdot M''} = M''\) if \(\overline{\mathfrak{a}\cdot M} = M\); but this implies \(M'' = 0\), since \(M''\) is a module of finite type over A (Nakayama); consequently, every open submodule \(M'\) of M is equal to M, which is therefore zero.

From Nakayama's lemma one draws the usual consequences: if \(\mathfrak{a}\) is a closed ideal contained in the radical of A and \(f : M \to N\) a morphism of pseudocompact A-modules, f is surjective if the induced map \(f'\) from \(M/\mathfrak{a}M\) to \(N/\mathfrak{a}N\) is (apply lemma 0.3.3 to \(\mathrm{Coker}\,f\)). Likewise, if N is projective, f is invertible if \(f'\) is (indeed f is surjective, hence possesses a section; one then applies 0.3.3 to \(\mathrm{Ker}\,f\)).

When A is local with maximal ideal \(\mathfrak{m}\), one can also deduce from 0.3.3 the following exchange theorem: if M is a topologically free A-module with pseudobasis \((m_i)_{i\in I}\) (0.2.1) and N a (closed) direct factor of M, there exists a pseudobasis of M formed of elements of N and of certain \(m_i\): indeed, this is clear when A is a field (then use the duality of 0.2.2 and apply the usual exchange theorem); consequently, \(N/\mathfrak{m}N\) has as complement a topologically free module over \(A/\mathfrak{m}\) with pseudobasis \((\overline{m_i})_{i\in J}\), where \(\overline{m_i}\) is the image of \(m_i\) in \(M/\mathfrak{m}M\) and J a subset of I. If P denotes the direct product \(\prod_{i\in J} Am_i\), the canonical map from \(N\oplus P\) to M is "bijective modulo \(\mathfrak{m}\)"; it is therefore bijective by what precedes (for another proof see C.A., IV, § 2, prop. 8).

Consider now three pseudocompact A-modules L, M and N, with N of finite length. Let \(\psi\) be an element of the set \(\mathrm{Hom}_c(L,\mathrm{Hom}_c(M,N))\) of continuous A-linear maps from L into the module \(\mathrm{Hom}_c(M,N)\) equipped with the discrete topology. Such a \(\psi\) defines a continuous bilinear map \(\psi' : (\ell,m) \mapsto \psi(\ell)(m)\) from \(L\times M\) to N. One thus obtains another characterization of \(\mathrm{Hom}_c(L \widehat{\otimes}_A M,N)\), which we shall use when M is the projective limit of a filtered projective system of pseudocompact A-modules \(M_i\). We then have natural isomorphisms \[ \begin{aligned} \mathrm{Hom}_c(L \widehat{\otimes}_A \varprojlim M_i, N) &\simeq \mathrm{Hom}_c(L, \mathrm{Hom}_c(\varprojlim M_i, N)) \\ &\simeq \mathrm{Hom}_c(L, \varinjlim \mathrm{Hom}_c(M_i,N)) \\ &\simeq \varinjlim \mathrm{Hom}_c(L, \mathrm{Hom}_c(M_i,N)) \\ &\simeq \varinjlim \mathrm{Hom}_c(L \widehat{\otimes}_A M_i, N) \\ &\simeq \mathrm{Hom}_c\Big(\varprojlim(L \widehat{\otimes}_A M_i), N\Big) \end{aligned} \] which allow us to identify \(L \widehat{\otimes}_A (\varprojlim M_i)\) with \(\varprojlim(L \widehat{\otimes}_A M_i)\).

In particular, the completed tensor product commutes with infinite products. For example, as the ring A is the product of its local components \(A_{\mathfrak{m}}\) (§ 0.1.1), every pseudocompact A-module M (\(\simeq A \widehat{\otimes}_A M\)) is identified with the product of the modules \(M_{\mathfrak{m}} = A_{\mathfrak{m}} \widehat{\otimes}_A M\) (the local components of M).

Likewise, let M and N be two pseudocompact A-modules and consider the map \(\varphi : M^*\otimes_A N^* \to (M \widehat{\otimes}_A N)^*\) (0.2.2) which associates to an element \(f\otimes g\) of \(M^*\otimes_A N^*\) the map \(m \widehat{\otimes} n \mapsto f(m)g(n)\) from \(M \widehat{\otimes}_A N\) to A. This map \(\varphi\) is bijective when M is isomorphic to A. As the functors \(M \mapsto M^*\otimes_A N^*\) and \(M \mapsto (M \widehat{\otimes}_A N)^*\) transform a direct product into a direct sum when A is artinian, one sees that \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism whenever A is artinian and M topologically free (or more generally projective).

For every closed maximal ideal \(\mathfrak{m}\) of A, the functor \(M \mapsto A_{\mathfrak{m}} \widehat{\otimes}_A M\) is obviously exact. As every projective pseudocompact A-module P is a product of modules of the form \(A_{\mathfrak{m}}\), it follows that the functor \(M \mapsto P \widehat{\otimes}_A M\) is exact when P is projective. The converse is true:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition

Let A be a pseudocompact ring, P a pseudocompact A-module. The functor \(M \mapsto P \widehat{\otimes}_A M\) is exact if and only if P is projective.

As \(P \widehat{\otimes}_A M\) is the product of its local components \[ (P \widehat{\otimes}_A M)_{\mathfrak{m}} \simeq P_{\mathfrak{m}} \widehat{\otimes}_{A_{\mathfrak{m}}} M_{\mathfrak{m}}, \] we are reduced to the case where the ring A is local. We shall then prove that P is topologically free: for this, let \(\mathfrak{m}\) be the maximal ideal of A; then \(P/\overline{\mathfrak{m}P}\) is a linearly compact vector space over \(A/\mathfrak{m}\), hence a product of copies of \(A/\mathfrak{m}\). There is therefore a family \((A_i)_{i\in I}\) of copies of A and an isomorphism \(\varphi : \prod_{i\in I}(A_i/\mathfrak{m}) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } P/\overline{\mathfrak{m}P}\). As \(\prod_{i\in I} A_i\) is projective, there is a commutative square \[ \xymatrix{ \prod_{i\in I} A_i \ar[r]^-{\psi} \ar[d]_-{p} & P \ar[d]^-{q} \\ \prod_{i\in I}(A_i/\mathfrak{m}) \ar[r]_-{\varphi}^-{\sim} & P/\overline{\mathfrak{m}P} } \] where p and q denote the canonical projections. Applying Nakayama's lemma to \(\mathrm{Coker}\,\psi\) and noting that \((A/\mathfrak{m}) \widehat{\otimes}_A \psi\) is none other than \(\varphi\), we see that \(\psi\) is surjective.

Setting then \(B = \prod_{i\in I} A_i\) and \(N = \mathrm{Ker}\,\psi\), we have the following commutative and exact diagram: \[ \xymatrix{ & & & & 0 \ar@{-->}[dddll] \\ & \mathfrak{m} \widehat{\otimes}_A N \ar[r] \ar[d] & \mathfrak{m} \widehat{\otimes}_A B \ar[r] \ar[d] & \mathfrak{m} \widehat{\otimes}_A P \ar[r] \ar[d] & 0 \\ 0 \ar[r] & A \widehat{\otimes}_A N \ar[r] \ar[d] & A \widehat{\otimes}_A B \ar[r]^-{\psi} \ar[d] & A \widehat{\otimes}_A P \ar[r] \ar[d] & 0 \\ & (A/\mathfrak{m}) \widehat{\otimes}_A N \ar[r] & (A/\mathfrak{m}) \widehat{\otimes}_A B \ar[r]^-{\varphi} & (A/\mathfrak{m}) \widehat{\otimes}_A P \ar[r] & 0 } \] The "snake lemma" applied to the first two rows then shows that the morphism \((A/\mathfrak{m}) \widehat{\otimes}_A N \to (A/\mathfrak{m}) \widehat{\otimes}_A B\) is a monomorphism. As \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism and the last row is exact, \((A/\mathfrak{m}) \widehat{\otimes}_A N\) is zero; whence \(N = 0\) by "Nakayama's lemma" 0.3.3. Therefore \(\psi\) is an isomorphism.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 0.3.8

Let A be a complete noetherian local ring and P a pseudocompact A-module. Then P is topologically free if and only if P is flat over A.

Indeed, the canonical map from \(M\otimes_A P\) to \(M \widehat{\otimes}_A P\) is bijective when M is equal to A, hence when M is noetherian (take a finite presentation of M and use the right exactness of the tensor product and of the completed tensor product). Now P is flat if and only if the functor \(M \mapsto M\otimes_A P\) is exact as M runs over the noetherian modules. Likewise, we saw in the proof of proposition 0.3.7 that P is topologically free if the sequence \[ 0 \longrightarrow \mathfrak{m} \widehat{\otimes}_A P \longrightarrow A \widehat{\otimes}_A P \longrightarrow (A/\mathfrak{m}) \widehat{\otimes}_A P \longrightarrow 0 \] is exact. Therefore P is topologically free if and only if the functor \(M \mapsto M \widehat{\otimes}_A P\) is exact as M runs over the noetherian modules. The corollary therefore follows from the equality \(M\otimes_A P = M \widehat{\otimes}_A P\) above.

Let k be a pseudocompact ring. A topological k-algebra A (with identity element and commutative) is said to be profinite if the underlying topological k-module is pseudocompact. If this is the case and if \(\mathfrak{l}\) is an open k-submodule of A, the composite map \[ \varphi : A\times A \xrightarrow{\ \mathrm{mult.}\ } A \xrightarrow{\ \mathrm{can.}\ } A/\mathfrak{l} \] is continuous. By lemma 0.3.1, there exists an open k-submodule \(\mathfrak{n}\) of A such that \(\varphi(A\times\mathfrak{n}) = 0\). This means that \(\mathfrak{l}\) contains the open ideal \(A\cdot\mathfrak{n}\) and implies that A is a pseudocompact ring.

Consider likewise a topological A-module M whose underlying k-module is pseudocompact. If \(M'\) is an open k-submodule of M, lemma 0.3.1 applied to the map \[ A\times M \xrightarrow{\ \mathrm{mult.}\ } M \xrightarrow{\ \mathrm{can.}\ } M/M' \] shows that \(M'\) contains an open A-submodule, hence that M is also a pseudocompact A-module.

If A and B are two profinite k-algebras, a morphism from A to B is, by definition, a continuous homomorphism of k-algebras. The category of profinite k-algebras obviously possesses projective limits: the algebra underlying a projective limit is the projective limit of the underlying algebras; the topology is that of the projective limit. Likewise, there are finite inductive limits: for example, if \(f : A \to B\) and \(g : A \to C\) are two morphisms of profinite k-algebras, the amalgamated sum of B and C under A is \(B \widehat{\otimes}_A C\) as underlying topological A-module (by 0.4, f and g equip B and C with structures of pseudocompact A-module); the multiplication of \(B \widehat{\otimes}_A C\) is obviously such that \((b\widehat{\otimes}c)(b'\widehat{\otimes}c') = (bb')\widehat{\otimes}(cc')\) if \(b,b'\in B\) and \(c,c'\in C\).
From now on we shall denote by \(\mathrm{Al}/k\) the category of profinite k-algebras.

A profinite k-algebra C will be said to be of finite length if the underlying k-module is of finite length (hence discrete); we shall denote by \(\mathrm{Alf}/k\) the full subcategory of \(\mathrm{Al}/k\) formed of the k-algebras of finite length and, for every profinite algebra A, \(\mathbf{h}^A\) will be the functor \(C \mapsto \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathrm{Al}/k}(A,C)\) from \(\mathrm{Alf}/k\) to the category \((\mathrm{Ens})\). It is clear that \(\mathbf{h}^A\) is a left exact functor; moreover, as \(\mathfrak{l}\) runs over the open ideals of A, the canonical projections of A onto \(A/\mathfrak{l}\) induce an isomorphism \[ \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathrm{Al}/k}(A,C) \xleftarrow{\ \sim\ } \varprojlim_{\mathfrak{l}} \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathrm{Alf}/k}(A/\mathfrak{l},C) \] whenever C is of finite length. This means that \(\mathbf{h}^A\) is the inductive limit of the representable functors \(\mathbf{h}^{A/\mathfrak{l}}\); if B is another profinite k-algebra, we thus have isomorphisms \[ \begin{aligned} \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathrm{Al}/k}(B,A) &\simeq \varprojlim_{\mathfrak{l}} \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathrm{Al}/k}(B,A/\mathfrak{l}) \simeq \varprojlim_{\mathfrak{l}} \mathbf{h}^B(A/\mathfrak{l}) \\ &\simeq \varprojlim_{\mathfrak{l}} \mathrm{Hom}(\mathbf{h}^{A/\mathfrak{l}}, \mathbf{h}^B) \simeq \mathrm{Hom}(\mathbf{h}^A, \mathbf{h}^B), \end{aligned} \] which show that the contravariant functor \(\mathbf{h}^? : A \mapsto \mathbf{h}^A\) is fully faithful.

In fact, \(\mathbf{h}^?\) is an antiequivalence from \(\mathrm{Al}/k\) to the category of left exact functors from \(\mathrm{Alf}/k\) to \((\mathrm{Ens})\). Indeed, by what precedes, it suffices to show that every left exact functor F is isomorphic to a functor of the type \(\mathbf{h}^A\); for this, A can be constructed as follows (TDTE II, § 3): as F is left exact, there is, for every k-algebra of finite length C and for every element \(\xi\) of \(F(C)\), a smallest subalgebra \(C'\) of C such that \(\xi\) belongs to the image of \(F(C')\) in \(F(C)\); if we have \(C' = C\), we say that the pair \((C,\xi)\) is minimal. The minimal pairs form a category if we take as morphisms from \((C,\xi)\) to \((D,\eta)\) the homomorphisms \(\varphi\) from C to D such that \((F\varphi)(\xi) = \eta\); it is clear that such a \(\varphi\) is a surjection and that the category of minimal pairs is "filtered on the left". Moreover, as we can manifestly restrict ourselves to the pairs \((C,\xi)\) such that C belongs to a set containing k-algebras of finite length of every type, the functor \((C,\xi) \mapsto C\), whose source category is that of minimal pairs and whose target category is that of profinite k-algebras, possesses a projective limit; we take for A this projective limit.

The existence of an antiequivalence from \(\mathrm{Al}/k\) to the category of left exact functors from \(\mathrm{Alf}/k\) to \((\mathrm{Ens})\) implies, for example, that \(\mathrm{Al}/k\) possesses infinite inductive limits: indeed, the category of left exact functors possesses projective limits, which are defined by the formula \[ (\varprojlim F_i)(C) = \varprojlim F_i(C), \] when C is a k-algebra of finite length and \((F_i)\) a projective system of functors.

Let k and \(\ell\) be pseudocompact rings and \(\varphi : k \to \ell\) a homomorphism (0.1.3). For every pseudocompact k-module M, we denote by \(M \widehat{\otimes}_k \ell\) the separated completion of \(M\otimes_k \ell\) for the linear topology defined by the sums of the images of \(M'\otimes_k \ell\) and \(M\otimes_k \ell'\) in \(M\otimes_k \ell\) (\((M',\ell')\) running over the pairs formed of an open submodule of M and an open ideal of \(\ell\)); likewise, if \(m\in M\) and \(x\in\ell\), we denote by \(m \widehat{\otimes}_k x\) the canonical image of \(m\otimes_k x\) in \(M \widehat{\otimes}_k \ell\). It is clear that \(M \widehat{\otimes}_k \ell\) is a pseudocompact \(\ell\)-module.

Associate to every pseudocompact \(\ell\)-module N and every morphism \(f : M\otimes_k \ell \to N\) the map \(f' : m \mapsto f(m \widehat{\otimes}_k 1)\) from M to N. It is clear that one thus obtains a bijection from \(\mathrm{Hom}_c(M \widehat{\otimes}_k \ell, N)\) onto the set of continuous k-linear maps from M into the k-module deduced from N by restriction of scalars. One deduces from this, as in 0.3.2 and 0.3.4, that the functor \(M \mapsto M \widehat{\otimes}_k \ell\) is right exact and that it commutes with infinite products and with the completed tensor product.

Finally, if A is a profinite k-algebra, there is on \(A \widehat{\otimes}_k \ell\) one and only one structure of profinite \(\ell\)-algebra such that \((m\widehat{\otimes}_k x)(n\widehat{\otimes}_k y) = (mn)\widehat{\otimes}_k(xy)\) if \(m,n\in A\) and \(x,y\in\ell\). We say that \(A \widehat{\otimes}_k \ell\) is the algebra deduced from A by the extension of scalars \(\varphi\). The functor \(A \to A \widehat{\otimes}_k \ell\) is right exact.

pages.title: 1. Formal varieties over a pseudocompact ring

blocks.html: 1. Formal varieties over a pseudocompact ring

To every pseudocompact ring A one can associate a formal scheme (EGA I, 10.4.2) by proceeding as follows: the underlying topological space is the set \(\Upsilon(A)\) of closed maximal ideals of A, equipped with the discrete topology; the structure sheaf has the cartesian product \(\prod_{\mathfrak{m}\in E} A_{\mathfrak{m}}\) as space of sections over a subset E of \(\Upsilon(A)\). The formal scheme thus obtained is denoted \(\mathrm{Spf}\,A\) (the formal spectrum of A).

If A and B are two pseudocompact rings, a morphism from \(\mathrm{Spf}\,B\) to \(\mathrm{Spf}\,A\) consists of the data of a map f from \(\Upsilon(B)\) to \(\Upsilon(A)\) and of a family of homomorphisms \(f_y\) (0.1.3) from \(A_{f(y)}\) to \(B_y\) (\(y\in\Upsilon(B)\)). Such a morphism induces a homomorphism from A (the product of the \(A_x\), \(x\in\Upsilon(A)\)) to B (the product of the \(B_y\)) and the converse is true: if \(\varphi : A \to B\) is a continuous homomorphism of algebras, the inverse image \(\varphi^{-1}(\mathfrak{n})\) of a closed (hence open) maximal ideal of B is an open prime ideal of A; it is therefore maximal in A and we may set \(\mathfrak{m} = \varphi^{-1}(\mathfrak{n}) = (\mathrm{Spf})(\mathfrak{n})\); the map \(\varphi\) then induces a homomorphism from \(A_{\mathfrak{m}}\) to \(B_{\mathfrak{n}}\).

We thus see that the contravariant functor \(A \mapsto \mathrm{Spf}\,A\) is fully faithful. Although we speak here only of formal schemes of the form \(\mathrm{Spf}\,A\), we shall use the language of formal schemes rather than that of pseudocompact rings in order to support our assertions with geometric intuition.

Let k be a pseudocompact ring. We shall call a formal variety over k any formal scheme X over \(\mathrm{Spf}\,k\) which is isomorphic to a formal k-scheme \(\mathrm{Spf}\,A\) for some profinite k-algebra A. The algebra A is then isomorphic to the affine algebra of X, that is to say to the algebra of sections of the structure sheaf \(\mathscr{O}_X\) of X. By 1.1 the functor \(A \mapsto \mathrm{Spf}\,A\) is an antiequivalence from \(\mathrm{Al}/k\) (0.4.1) to the full subcategory \(\mathbf{Vf}/k\) of the category of formal schemes over \(\mathrm{Spf}\,k\) whose objects are the formal k-varieties.

The category \(\mathbf{Vf}/k\) possesses projective and inductive limits: let for example Y, Z be formal k-varieties over the same formal k-variety X and let A, B, C be the affine algebras of X, Y, Z; by 0.4.1, the affine algebra of the fibered product \(Y\times_X Z\) is identified with \(B \widehat{\otimes}_A C\), so that the inclusion of \(\mathbf{Vf}/k\) into the category of all formal k-schemes commutes with finite projective limits (cf. EGA I, 10.7). Likewise, the inductive limits of formal k-varieties correspond to the projective limits of their affine algebras: let, for example, \(d,e : X \rightrightarrows Y\) be a double arrow of \(\mathbf{Vf}/k\); the affine algebra of \(\mathrm{Coker}(d,e)\) is isomorphic to the kernel of the homomorphisms induced on the affine algebras of X and Y; but one can also give the following construction of \(\mathrm{Coker}(d,e)\): the set underlying \(\mathrm{Coker}(d,e)\) is the cokernel of the underlying sets; if p is the canonical projection of the set underlying Y onto the cokernel and if z belongs to the cokernel, the local algebra of \(\mathrm{Coker}(d,e)\) at z is the kernel of the double arrow \[ d',e' : \prod_{p(y)=z} \mathscr{O}_{Y,y} \rightrightarrows \prod_{q(x)=z} \mathscr{O}_{X,x} \] where we have set \(q = pd = pe\) and where \(d'\) and \(e'\) are induced by the homomorphisms \(d_x\) and \(e_x\) (notation of 1.1).

Finally, if \(\varphi : k \to \ell\) is a homomorphism of pseudocompact rings and X a formal k-variety with affine algebra A, the formal scheme \(X \times_{\mathrm{Spf}\,k} \mathrm{Spf}\,\ell\), obtained by base change, is a formal \(\ell\)-variety, which we shall also denote \(X \widehat{\otimes}_k \ell\) and which has for affine algebra the completed tensor product \(A \widehat{\otimes}_k \ell\) (confer 0.5 and EGA I, 10).
As every formal variety over k decomposes into formal varieties over the local components of k, we shall assume in certain proofs that k is a local pseudocompact ring.
Let us now give a few examples, at the same time fixing our terminology:

A k-functor will be, by definition, a covariant functor from \(\mathrm{Alf}/k\) to \((\mathrm{Ens})\). By 0.4.2, we may identify \(\mathbf{Vf}/k\) with a full subcategory of \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(\mathrm{Alf}/k,(\mathrm{Ens}))\), by associating to every formal k-variety the functor \(C \mapsto X(C)\) (C runs over the objects of \(\mathrm{Alf}/k\) and \(X(C)\) denotes the set \(\mathrm{Hom}_{\mathbf{Vf}/k}(\mathrm{Spf}\,C,X)\)).

We shall encounter further on functors \(\mathbf{F}\) which associate to every object C of \(\mathrm{Alf}/k\) a module \(\mathbf{F}(C)\) over C and to every morphism \(\varphi : C \to D\) of \(\mathrm{Alf}/k\) a linear map \(\mathbf{F}(\varphi) : \mathbf{F}(C) \to \mathbf{F}(D)\) such that \((\mathbf{F}(\varphi))(\lambda x) = \varphi(\lambda)\cdot(\mathbf{F}(\varphi)(x))\), if \(x\in\mathbf{F}(C)\) and \(\lambda\in C\).

By I 3.1, \(\mathbf{F}\) is equipped with a structure of \(\mathbf{O}_k\)-module, if we denote by \(\mathbf{O}_k\) the ring-valued k-functor which associates to every object C of \(\mathrm{Alf}/k\) the ring underlying C. A k-module \(\mathbf{F}\) will be said to be flat if \(\mathbf{F}(C)\) is flat over C for every object C of \(\mathrm{Alf}/k\), and if every morphism \(\varphi : C \to D\) of \(\mathrm{Alf}/k\) induces a bijection from \(D\otimes_C \mathbf{F}(C)\) onto \(\mathbf{F}(D)\).

For example, if M is a module over k, we shall denote by \(\underline{M}\) the \(\mathbf{O}_k\)-module \(C \mapsto C\otimes_k M\); then \(\underline{M}\) is flat when M is flat over k. Moreover, the functor \(M \mapsto \underline{M}\) has as right adjoint the functor which associates to every \(\mathbf{O}_k\)-module \(\mathbf{F}\) the projective limit \(\varprojlim_{\mathfrak{l}} \mathbf{F}(k/\mathfrak{l})\), \(\mathfrak{l}\) running over the open ideals of k.

In what follows, an \(\mathbf{O}_k\)-module will always be denoted by a boldface letter such as \(\mathbf{F}\); when k is artinian, we shall then write simply F instead of \(\mathbf{F}(k)\). In this case, it goes without saying that the functor \(\mathbf{F} \mapsto F\) induces an equivalence from the category of flat \(\mathbf{O}_k\)-modules to that of flat k-modules! The terminology we have adopted therefore has the sole purpose of allowing us to reason "as if k were always artinian".

In accordance with exposé I § 3.1, we shall use analogous conventions for other algebraic structures: thus, an \(\mathbf{O}_k\)-algebra (resp. an \(\mathbf{O}_k\)-coalgebra, resp. an \(\mathbf{O}_k\)-Lie algebra, resp. a p-Lie algebra over \(\mathbf{O}_k\)) will consist of the data of an \(\mathbf{O}_k\)-module M and, for every object C of \(\mathrm{Alf}/k\), of a structure of C-algebra (resp. of C-coalgebra, resp. of C-Lie algebra, resp. of p-Lie algebra over C) on \(\mathbf{M}(C)\); one assumes moreover that, for every morphism \(\varphi : C \to D\) of \(\mathrm{Alf}/k\), the map from \(D\otimes_C \mathbf{M}(C)\) to \(\mathbf{M}(D)\) induced by \(\mathbf{M}(\varphi)\) is a homomorphism of D-algebras (resp. of C-coalgebras, resp. …).

Let us finally note that, if \(\mathbf{F}\) and \(\mathbf{G}\) are two \(\mathbf{O}_k\)-modules, \(\mathbf{F}\otimes_k \mathbf{G}\) will denote the \(\mathbf{O}_k\)-module \(C \mapsto \mathbf{F}(C)\otimes_C \mathbf{G}(C)\).

Let N be a pseudocompact k-module. We denote by \(\mathbf{V}_k^f(N)\) or \(\mathbf{N}^*\) the \(\mathbf{O}_k\)-module which associates to every \(C\in\mathrm{Alf}/k\) the C-module \((C \widehat{\otimes}_k N)^*\) dual to \(C \widehat{\otimes}_k N\) (0.2.2); this \(\mathbf{O}_k\)-module \(\mathbf{N}^*\) will be called the dual of N. It is clear that \(\mathbf{N}^*\) is flat (1.2.1) when N is a projective pseudocompact k-module.

Conversely, if M is an \(\mathbf{O}_k\)-module, we call the dual of M, and denote by \(M^*\), the projective limit of the pseudocompact modules \((\mathbf{M}(k/\ell))^*\) over \(k/\ell\) (\(\ell\) runs over the open ideals of k). If \(\mathbf{M}\) is a flat \(\mathbf{O}_k\)-module, it is clear that \(\mathbf{M}\) is none other than the dual of \(M^*\), hence that the functors \(N \mapsto \mathbf{N}^*\) and \(\mathbf{M} \mapsto M^*\) define an antiequivalence between the category of projective pseudocompact k-modules and that of flat \(\mathbf{O}_k\)-modules (confer 0.2.2).

Moreover, if M is a topologically free pseudocompact k-module, let \((m_i)\) be a pseudobasis of M (0.2.1) and \(m_i^C\) the canonical image of \(m_i\) in \(C \widehat{\otimes}_k M\) (\(C\in\mathrm{Alf}/k\)). If we define the element \(n_i^C\) of \((C \widehat{\otimes}_k M)^*\) by the equalities \(n_i^C(m_i^C) = 1\) and \(n_i^C(m_j^C) = 0\) for \(i\neq j\), the family \((n_i^C)\) is a basis of \(\mathbf{M}^*(C)\); furthermore, for every morphism \(\varphi : C \to D\) of \(\mathrm{Alf}/k\), \(\mathbf{M}^*(\varphi)\) sends \(n_i^C\) to \(n_i^D\): one can therefore choose bases for the C-modules \(\mathbf{M}^*(C)\) "in a coherent manner".

For every pseudocompact k-module E, the \(\mathbf{O}_k\)-module \(\mathbf{E}^*\) is naturally isomorphic to the functor \(C \mapsto \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathrm{Al}/k}(\widehat{S}_k(E),C)\), where \(\widehat{S}_k(E)\) denotes the completed symmetric algebra of E, which can be constructed in the following manner: let \(T_k(E)\) be the direct sum of the abelian groups \[ \widehat{\bigotimes}{}_k^n E = E \widehat{\otimes}_k \cdots \widehat{\otimes}_k E \quad (n\geqslant 0\ ;\ \text{if } n=0,\ \widehat{\bigotimes}{}_k^0 E = k)\text{;} \] one makes \(T_k(E)\) into a graded k-algebra by defining the multiplication in the usual way; let then \(S_k(E)\) be the quotient of \(T_k(E)\) by the homogeneous ideal whose n-th component is the closed k-submodule of \(\widehat{\bigotimes}{}_k^n E\) generated by the elements \[ x_1 \widehat{\otimes} \cdots \widehat{\otimes} x_i \widehat{\otimes} x_{i+1} \widehat{\otimes} \cdots \widehat{\otimes} x_n - x_1 \widehat{\otimes} \cdots \widehat{\otimes} x_{i+1} \widehat{\otimes} x_i \widehat{\otimes} \cdots \widehat{\otimes} x_n. \]

If \(S_k^n(E)\) is the quotient of \(\otimes_k^n E\) by this submodule, \(S_k(E)\) is obviously a graded k-algebra with n-th component \(S_k^n(E)\); the profinite algebra \(\widehat{S}_k(E)\) is the separated completion of \(S_k(E)\) for the linear topology defined by the ideals \(\mathfrak{l}\) such that \(S_k(E)/\mathfrak{l}\) is a k-module of finite length and \(S_k^n(E)\cap\mathfrak{l}\) is an open submodule of \(S_k^n(E)\) for every n.
In all that follows, we identify the formal spectrum of \(\widehat{S}_k(E)\) with the k-functor \(\mathbf{V}_k(E)\) (1.2.3).

By 1.2.4, the zero morphism from E to k is associated to a morphism of profinite algebras \(\pi : \widehat{S}_k(E) \to k\); this morphism \(\pi\) induces the zero map on \(S_k^n(E)\) for \(n\geqslant 1\) and defines a section of the canonical projection of \(\mathbf{V}_k^f(E)\) onto \(\mathrm{Spf}\,k\). We shall denote by \(\mathbf{V}_k^{f,0}(E)\) the formal variety whose points are the images x of the points s of \(\mathrm{Spf}\,k\) under the section \(\mathrm{Spf}\,\pi\) and which has the same local algebras as \(\mathbf{V}_k^f(E)\) at these points x. It is clear that the affine algebra of \(\mathbf{V}_k^{f,0}(E)\) is the infinite product \[ k[[E]] = k \times E \times S_k^2(E) \times S_k^3(E) \times \cdots. \]

The multiplication of \(k[[E]]\) is induced by that of \(S_k(E)\) and we have a natural isomorphism \[ \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathrm{Al}/k}(k[[E]],C) \simeq \mathrm{Hom}_c(E,\mathfrak{r}(C)) \] when C runs over the k-algebras of finite length and \(\mathfrak{r}(C)\) denotes the radical of C.

A formal k-variety V is said to be of finite length if its affine algebra is. Likewise, if S is a scheme, an S-scheme X is said to be of finite length if X is finite over S and if the direct image of \(\mathscr{O}_X\) on S is an \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-Module of finite length. The S-schemes of finite length are identified with the formal varieties of finite length over the following formal scheme \(\widehat{S}\): the topological space underlying \(\widehat{S}\) is the set of closed points of S equipped with the discrete topology; if s is such a closed point, the structure sheaf \(\mathscr{O}_{\widehat{S}}\) has for stalk \(\mathscr{O}_{\widehat{S},s}\) at s the separated completion of \(\mathscr{O}_{S,s}\) for the linear topology defined by the ideals of finite colength.

Let \(\mathbf{V}/\widehat{S}\) be the category of formal varieties of finite length over \(\widehat{S}\) (identified with that of S-schemes of finite length). By 1.1 and 1.2.1, the category \(\mathbf{Vf}/\widehat{S}\) of formal varieties over \(\widehat{S}\) is equivalent to that of left exact contravariant functors from \(\mathbf{V}/\widehat{S}\) to \((\mathrm{Ens})\). For example, if X is an S-scheme, the functor \(T \mapsto \mathrm{Hom}_{(\mathrm{Sch}/S)}(T,X)\) is such a left exact functor; it is therefore naturally isomorphic to a functor \(T \mapsto \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathbf{Vf}/\widehat{S}}(T,\widehat{X/S})\), where \(\widehat{X/S}\) is a formal variety over \(\widehat{S}\) which can be described in the following manner: the underlying set is formed of the points x of X such that the residue field \(k(x)\) of X at x is a finite extension of the residue field \(k(s)\) of the image s of x in S; the stalk of \(\mathscr{O}_{\widehat{X/S}}\) at x is the separated completion of the local ring \(\mathscr{O}_{X,x}\) for the linear topology defined by the ideals of finite colength.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 1.3

Let \(f : X \to Y\) be a morphism of formal varieties over k, A and B the affine algebras of X and Y, \(g : B \to A\) the morphism induced by f. Then f is a monomorphism of \(\mathbf{Vf}/k\) if and only if g is a surjection.

Indeed, A is a pseudocompact k-module and g makes A into a pseudocompact B-module (0.4); it is therefore permissible to assume B equal to k. Moreover, as B and A are the products of the local components \(B_{\mathfrak{n}}\) and \(A_{\mathfrak{n}}\) (\(\mathfrak{n}\in\Upsilon(B)\)), we may assume B local. If \(\mathfrak{n}\) is then the maximal ideal of B, it suffices to show that \(g \widehat{\otimes}_B(B/\mathfrak{n})\) is surjective (Nakayama's lemma, 0.3.3); now \(g \widehat{\otimes}_B(B/\mathfrak{n})\) is deduced from g by base change, hence is an epimorphism of \(\mathrm{Al}/k\). We may therefore assume that \(B=k\) is a field. Now f is a monomorphism if and only if the diagonal morphism \(X \to X\times_Y X\) is an isomorphism, that is to say if the homomorphism \(x \widehat{\otimes}_B x' \mapsto xx'\) is an isomorphism from \(A \widehat{\otimes}_k A\) onto A. As k is a field, this implies \(A=k\).

The preceding proposition implies in particular that every monomorphism of \(\mathbf{Vf}/k\) is effective (confer IV 1.3). The same does not hold for epimorphisms, as one easily sees by slightly modifying the counterexample of V 2c); this is why we shall consider a pleasant class of effective epimorphisms:

A morphism \(f : X \to Y\) of formal k-varieties is said to be surjective if it induces a surjection of the underlying sets; it is said to be topologically flat if, for every point x of X, the homomorphism \(f_x : \mathscr{O}_{Y,f(x)} \to \mathscr{O}_{X,x}\) induced by f makes \(\mathscr{O}_{X,x}\) into a topologically free module over \(\mathscr{O}_{Y,f(x)}\).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition

A surjective and topologically flat morphism of formal k-varieties is an effective epimorphism (IV 1.3).

Indeed, let A and B be the affine algebras of X, Y and let \(g : B \to A\) be the morphism induced by f. By what we saw in 1.2, we may assume B local. Our hypothesis then means that g makes A into a topologically free and nonzero B-module. If \(\mathfrak{n}\) is the maximal ideal of B, \(A/\mathfrak{n}A\) is therefore not zero, so that the map \(g' : B/\mathfrak{n} \to A/\mathfrak{n}A\) deduced from g is an injection. By lemma 1.3.2 below, the B-module A is therefore identified with the direct sum of B and a complement \(A'\) of the image of B in A. It obviously follows that g is an isomorphism from B onto the part of A formed of the a such that \(a \widehat{\otimes}_B 1 = 1 \widehat{\otimes}_B a\), qed

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.3.2

Let B be a local pseudocompact ring with maximal ideal \(\mathfrak{n}\), M and N two projective pseudocompact B-modules and g a morphism from M to N. Then g is an isomorphism from M onto a direct factor of N if \((B/\mathfrak{n}) \widehat{\otimes}_B g\) is an injection.

Indeed, set \(g' = (B/\mathfrak{n}) \widehat{\otimes}_B g\). Then \(g'\) possesses a retraction \(r'\). If p and q are the canonical projections of M and N onto \(M/\mathfrak{n}M\) and \(N/\mathfrak{n}N\), there is therefore a morphism r such that \(pr = r'q\); consequently, \(r'\) is deduced from r by passage to the quotient and \(rg\) is an isomorphism because \(r'g'\) is (0.3.4). If s is the inverse isomorphism of \(rg\), then g has \(sr\) as retraction.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 1.3.3

Let \(f : X \to Y\) and \(g : Y \to Z\) be morphisms of formal k-varieties:

  1. (i)

    If f and g are topologically flat, so is \(g\circ f\).

  2. (ii)

    If g and \(g\circ f\) are topologically flat and if f is surjective, then g is topologically flat.

  3. (iii)

    If f is topologically flat, so is \(f\times_Y Y'\) for every base change \(Y' \to Y\).

Assertions (i) and (iii) are clear. To prove (ii), call A, B, C the affine algebras of X, Y, Z, and \(f' : B \to A\) and \(g' : C \to B\) the morphisms induced by f and g. As \(g\circ f\) is topologically flat, \(f'\circ g'\) makes A into a projective pseudocompact C-module; likewise, \(f'\) makes A into a faithful pseudocompact B-module. When P runs over the pseudocompact C-modules and N over the pseudocompact B-modules, the functors \(P \mapsto P \widehat{\otimes}_C A\) and \(N \mapsto N \widehat{\otimes}_B A\) are therefore exact; as the second is moreover faithful, the functor \(P \mapsto P \widehat{\otimes}_C B\) is exact; by 0.3.7, B is therefore a projective pseudocompact C-module.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 1.3.4

Let S be a scheme, Y a locally noetherian S-scheme and \(f : X \to Y\) an S-morphism locally of finite type and faithfully flat. Then \(\widehat{f/S} : \widehat{X/S} \to \widehat{Y/S}\) is a surjective and topologically flat morphism of formal \(\widehat{S}\)-varieties (1.2.6). Moreover, the sequence \[ \xymatrix{ \widehat{(X\times_Y X)/S} \ar@<0.6ex>[r]^-{\widehat{\mathrm{pr}_1/S}} \ar@<-0.6ex>[r]_-{\widehat{\mathrm{pr}_2/S}} & \widehat{X/S} \ar[r]^-{\widehat{f/S}} & \widehat{Y/S} } \] deduced from the exact sequence \[ \xymatrix{ X\times_Y X \ar@<0.6ex>[r]^-{\mathrm{pr}_1} \ar@<-0.6ex>[r]_-{\mathrm{pr}_2} & X \ar[r]^-{f} & Y } \] is exact.

Indeed, let y be a point of Y with projection s on S and such that \(k(y)\) is a finite extension of the residue field \(k(s)\) of s. As f is locally of finite type, \(f^{-1}(y)\) is locally of finite type over \(k(y)\) and the points of \(\widehat{X/S}\) projecting to y are the closed points of \(f^{-1}(y)\); this shows that f is surjective.

If x is a closed point of \(f^{-1}(y)\), the local rings of x and y on \(\widehat{X/S}\) and \(\widehat{Y/S}\) are the completions of \(\mathscr{O}_{X,x}\) and \(\mathscr{O}_{Y,y}\) for the linear topologies defined by the powers of the maximal ideals. By SGA IV 5.8, \(\widehat{\mathscr{O}}_{X,x}\) is therefore flat over \(\widehat{\mathscr{O}}_{Y,y}\). By 0.3.8, \(\widehat{\mathscr{O}}_{X,x}\) is a topologically free \(\widehat{\mathscr{O}}_{Y,y}\)-module.

Finally, we saw in 1.2.4 that the functor \(T \to \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathbf{Vf}/\widehat{S}}(T,\widehat{X/S})\) is naturally isomorphic to the functor \(T \mapsto \mathrm{Hom}_{(\mathrm{Sch}/S)}(T,X)\) (T being of finite length). As a projective limit of left exact functors is computed "argument by argument" and \(\mathbf{Vf}/\widehat{S}\) is a category equivalent to that of left exact contravariant functors from \(\mathbf{V}/\widehat{S}\) to \((\mathrm{Ens})\), we see that the functor \(X \mapsto \widehat{X/S}\) commutes with projective limits. In particular \(\widehat{(X\times_Y X)/S}\) is identified with \(\widehat{X/S} \times_{\widehat{Y/S}} \widehat{X/S}\), so that our last assertion says simply that \(\widehat{f/S}\) is an effective epimorphism (1.3.1).

A formal variety X over k is said to be topologically flat if its affine algebra A is a projective pseudocompact k-module, that is to say if the structural morphism \(X \to \mathrm{Spf}(k)\) is topologically flat. One can describe such a formal variety by means of coalgebras: indeed, denote by \(\mathbf{H}(X)\) the \(\mathbf{O}_k\)-module \(\mathbf{V}_k^f(A)\) dual to A (1.2.3). As \(\mathbf{V}^f(A\widehat{\otimes}A)\) is identified with \(\mathbf{V}^f(A)\otimes_k \mathbf{V}^f(A)\) (1.2.2) by 0.3.6, the multiplication of the algebra A induces by transposition a diagonal morphism, which makes \(\mathbf{H}(X)\) into an \(\mathbf{O}_k\)-coalgebra (1.2.2). We shall say that \(\mathbf{H}(X)\) is the coalgebra of X over \(\mathbf{O}_k\).

Conversely, every \(\mathbf{O}_k\)-coalgebra \(\mathbf{H}\) defines a k-functor (1.2.1) \(\mathrm{Spf}^*\mathbf{H}\): for every object C of \(\mathrm{Alf}/k\), \((\mathrm{Spf}^*\mathbf{H})(C)\) is the set of elements x of \(\mathbf{H}(C)\) such that \(\varepsilon_{\mathbf{H}(C)}(x) = 1\) and \(\Delta_{\mathbf{H}(C)}(x) = x\otimes x\) (notation of VIIA 3.1). When the \(\mathbf{O}_k\)-module underlying \(\mathbf{H}\) is flat, \(\mathrm{Spf}^*\mathbf{H}\) is identified with the formal spectrum of an algebra \(H^*\in\mathrm{Al}/k\), whose underlying topological k-module is the dual of H (1.2.3), the multiplication of \(H^*\) being induced by the diagonal morphisms of the coalgebras \(\mathbf{H}(C)\) (confer VIIA 3.1.2). One thus sees that the functors \(X \mapsto \mathbf{H}(X)\) and \(\mathbf{H} \mapsto \mathrm{Spf}^*\mathbf{H}\) define an equivalence between the category of topologically flat formal k-varieties and that of flat \(\mathbf{O}_k\)-coalgebras.

In the remainder of this exposé, we shall several times define a topologically flat formal variety X by exhibiting the coalgebra \(\mathbf{H}(X)\). We shall then have to interpret by means of \(\mathbf{H}(X)\) certain geometric properties of X. We give here an example of this situation: suppose for simplicity that k is artinian and set \(H = \mathbf{H}(X)(k)\); suppose given moreover a section \(\sigma\) of the canonical projection of X onto \(\mathrm{Spf}\,k\) and ask under what condition \(\sigma\) induces an isomorphism of the underlying topological spaces: for this, denote by \(H^+\) the kernel of the augmentation \(\varepsilon_H\) (VIIA 3.1), and by \(\eta_H\) the section of \(\varepsilon_H\) induced by \(\sigma\). The map \((x,y) \mapsto \eta_H(x)+y\) then allows us to identify \(k\oplus H^+\) with H and to define by induction submodules \(H_n\) of H: \[ \begin{aligned} H_0 &= \eta_H(k)\\ H_{n+1} &= \{u\in H \mid \Delta_H u - u\otimes 1 \in H_n\otimes H^+\}. \end{aligned} \]

We shall say that the sequence \(H_0\subset H_1\subset\cdots\) is the natural filtration of H; under the hypotheses above, one then sees easily that the section \(\sigma\) of the canonical projection of X onto k induces an isomorphism on the underlying topological spaces if and only if H is the union of the \(H_n\): indeed, let A be the affine algebra of X, \(\varepsilon_A : A \to k\) the homomorphism induced by \(\sigma\) and I the kernel of \(\varepsilon_A\), that is to say the orthogonal of \(H_0\) in A. Denote moreover by \(A'\) (resp. \(A''\)) the ring of sections of the structure sheaf of X over the image of \(\sigma\) (resp. over the complement of this image in X). The algebra A is then canonically isomorphic to \(A'\oplus A''\) and I is identified with a subset of \(A'\oplus A''\) of the form \(I'\oplus A''\). As \(A''\) is a projective pseudocompact k-module and the ideals \(I'^r\) tend to 0, it is clear that \(A''\) is zero if and only if every continuous linear map from A to k vanishes on an ideal \(I^{n+1}\), hence if and only if H is the union of the k-modules \((A/I^{n+1})^*\). Let us show that \(H_n = (A/I^{n+1})^*\):

This is clear for \(n=0\). Suppose it therefore verified for \(n<r\): to the exact sequences \[ 0 \longrightarrow \overline{I^r} \longrightarrow A \longrightarrow A/I^r \longrightarrow 0 \] and \[ \overline{I^r} \widehat{\otimes} I \longrightarrow A \longrightarrow A/I^{r+1} \longrightarrow 0 \] there correspond by duality the exact sequences \[ (\overline{I^r})^* \longleftarrow H \longleftarrow H_{r-1} \longleftarrow 0 \] \[ \text{and}\qquad (\overline{I^r})^*\otimes H^+ \xleftarrow{\ \delta\ } H \longleftarrow (A/\overline{I^{r+1}})^* \longleftarrow 0, \] where \(\delta\) is obtained by composing \(\Delta_H\) with the canonical projection of \(H\otimes H\) onto \((\overline{I^r})^*\otimes H^+\). The first sequence shows that \(H/H_{r-1}\) injects into \((\overline{I^r})^*\), hence that \((H/H_{r-1})\otimes H^+\) injects into \((\overline{I^r})^*\otimes H^+\) (\(H^+\) is flat). On the other hand, \(\Delta_H u - u\otimes 1\) belongs to \(H\otimes H^+\) for every \(u\in H\), hence is the projection of u onto \(H\otimes H^+\). The second exact sequence then shows that u belongs to \((A/\overline{I^{r+1}})^*\) if and only if \(\Delta_H u - u\otimes 1\) belongs to the kernel of the canonical map from \(H\otimes H^+\) onto \((H/H_{r-1})\otimes H^+\), that is to say to \(H_{r-1}\otimes H^+\).

blocks.title: Théorème
blocks.html:
Theorem 1.4

Let k be a pseudocompact ring and \(d_0,d_1 : X_1 \rightrightarrows X\) an equivalence pair of \(\mathbf{Vf}/k\) (V 2b) such that \(d_1\) is topologically flat. Then the canonical projection of X onto \(X/X_1\) (\(=\mathrm{Coker}(d_0,d_1)\)) is surjective and topologically flat, and the morphism \(X_1 \to X\times_{X/X_1} X\) with components \(d_0\) and \(d_1\) is an isomorphism.
The proof of this theorem occupies paragraphs 1.4.1, 1.4.2 and 1.4.3:

Let us first show that we can reduce to the case where X has a single point: as we are dealing with an equivalence pair, one sees as in V 3e that one defines an equivalence relation on the set underlying X by declaring that two points x, y are equivalent if there exists a point z of \(X_1\) such that \(d_0z=y\) and \(d_1z=x\). We may obviously assume without inconvenience that X contains a single equivalence class for this relation, in other words that \(X/X_1\) has a single point (see the construction of \(X/X_1\) given in 1.2): in this case, let x be an arbitrary point of X and U the formal variety having x as its only point and having the same local ring as X at x. One then sees as in V § 6 that the equivalence relation induced by \((d_0,d_1)\) on U still satisfies the hypotheses of the theorem and that it suffices to carry out the proof for this latter equivalence relation (U is a "quasi-section"):

Let us briefly recall the principle of the proof given in V, § 6: set \(V = d_0^{-1}(U) = U\times_{i,d_0} X_1\), where i is the inclusion of U in X; let u and v be the morphisms with source V induced respectively by \(d_0\) and \(d_1\): \[ X \xleftarrow{\ v\ } V \xrightarrow{\ u\ } U. \]

It is clear that u and v are topologically flat and that u is surjective; as X contains a single equivalence class, v is surjective. If \((v_0,v_1)\) is the inverse image of the equivalence pair \((d_0,d_1)\) under v (V 3a), it follows from V § 3c d that \(X/X_1\) and the quotient of U by the equivalence relation induced by \((d_0,d_1)\) are both identified with \(\mathrm{Coker}(v_0,v_1)\). The rest is easy (V § 6).

We are thus reduced to the case where X has a single point: consider then the following commutative diagram (confer V § 1 (0,1,2)) \[ \xymatrix{ X_2 \ar@<0.6ex>[r]^-{d_1'} \ar@<-0.6ex>[r]_-{d_0'} \ar[d]_-{d_2'} & X_1 \ar[r]^-{d_0} \ar[d]^-{d_1} & X \ar[d] \\ X_1 \ar@<0.6ex>[r]^-{d_1} \ar@<-0.6ex>[r]_-{d_0} & X \ar[r] & X/X_1 } \] where \(X_2\) is the fibered product \(X_1\times_{d_1,d_0} X_1\), with \(d_2',d_1'\) and \(d_0'\) being respectively the morphisms "\((x,y,z)\mapsto(x,y)\)", "\((x,y,z)\mapsto(x,z)\)" and "\((x,y,z)\mapsto(y,z)\)". If B, A, \(A_1\) and \(A_2\) denote respectively the affine algebras of \(X/X_1\), X, \(X_1\) and \(X_2\), the preceding diagram induces a commutative diagram \[ \xymatrix{ A_2 \ar@<0.6ex>[r]^-{j_1} \ar@<-0.6ex>[r]_-{j_0} \ar[d]_-{j_2} & A_1 \ar[d]^-{i_1} & A \ar[l]_-{i_0} \ar[d]^-{i} \\ A_1 \ar@<0.6ex>[r]^-{i_1} \ar@<-0.6ex>[r]_-{i_0} & A & B \ar[l]^-{i} } \] in which the two rows are exact and the squares determined by \((i_0,j_0)\) and \((i_1,j_1)\) are cocartesian. As the morphism \(X_1 \to X\times X\) with components \(d_0\) and \(d_1\) is a monomorphism by hypothesis, the morphism \(A\widehat{\otimes}_k A \to A_1\) with components \(i_0\) and \(i_1\) is surjective (1.3). This means that \(i_1\) makes \(A_1\) into a pseudocompact A-module (assumed topologically free) generated by \(i_0(A)\). As A is local, lemma 1.4.3 below implies the existence of a topologically free k-module V and of a morphism of pseudocompact k-modules \(j : V \to A\) such that the morphism \(\alpha_1 : a\widehat{\otimes}v \mapsto i_1(a)\cdot i_0(jv)\) from \(A\widehat{\otimes}_k V\) to \(A_1\) is invertible. Let \(\alpha : B\widehat{\otimes}_k V \to A\) and \(\alpha_2 : A_1\widehat{\otimes}_k V \to A_2\) be the morphisms "\(b\widehat{\otimes}v \mapsto b\cdot j(v)\)" and "\(a\otimes v \mapsto (j_2a)\cdot j_0(i_0(jv))\)". In the following commutative diagram \[ \xymatrix{ A_2 \ar@<0.6ex>[r]^-{j_1} \ar@<-0.6ex>[r]_-{j_0} & A_1 & A \ar[l]_-{i_0} \\ A_1\widehat{\otimes}_k V \ar@<0.6ex>[r]^-{i_1\otimes V} \ar@<-0.6ex>[r]_-{i_0\otimes V} \ar[u]^-{\alpha_2} & A\widehat{\otimes}_k V \ar[u]^-{\alpha_1} & B\widehat{\otimes}_k V \ar[l]^-{i\otimes V} \ar[u]_-{\alpha} } \] the two rows are therefore exact and the two left-hand squares are cocartesian. As \(\alpha_1\) is invertible, the same holds for \(\alpha_2\), hence for \(\alpha\). This shows on the one hand that A is topologically free over B with "basis j" and that one obtains a pseudobasis of \(A_1\) over A (regarded as an A-module by means of \(i_1\); 0.2.1) by taking the image under \(i_0\) of a pseudobasis of A over B; this implies that the morphism \(A\widehat{\otimes}_B A \to A_1\) with components \(i_1\) and \(i_0\) is invertible: \[ A\widehat{\otimes}_B A \simeq A\widehat{\otimes}_B B\widehat{\otimes}_k V \simeq A\widehat{\otimes}_k V \simeq A_1. \]

blocks.title: Lemme
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.4.3

Let k be a local pseudocompact ring, A a local profinite k-algebra, \(A_1\) a topologically free A-module and \(i_0 : M \to A_1\) a morphism of pseudocompact k-modules such that the map \(a\widehat{\otimes}m \mapsto a\cdot i_0(m)\) from \(A\widehat{\otimes}_k M\) to \(A_1\) is surjective. There then exist a topologically free k-module V and a morphism of pseudocompact k-modules \(j : V \to M\) such that the map \(a\widehat{\otimes}v \mapsto a\cdot i_0(jv)\) from \(A\widehat{\otimes}_k V\) to \(A_1\) is bijective.

As every pseudocompact k-module is the quotient of a topologically free k-module, we may assume without inconvenience that M is topologically free over k; so take for M the direct product of a family \((M_i)_{i\in I}\) of copies of k. In this case, \(A\widehat{\otimes}_k M\) is none other than the product \(\prod_{i\in I} A\widehat{\otimes}_k M_i\), each factor of which is isomorphic to A, hence is projective and indecomposable. As the map \(a\widehat{\otimes}m \mapsto a\cdot i_0(m)\) is surjective and \(A_1\) is projective, the kernel of this map is a direct factor of \(A\widehat{\otimes}_k M\); it therefore follows from the exchange theorem (0.3.4) that this kernel has as complement a partial product \(\prod_{i\in J} A\widehat{\otimes}_k M_i\), where J denotes a certain subset of I.

Let k be a pseudocompact ring and \((f_i)\) a family of morphisms \(f_i : X_i \to X\) of \(\mathbf{Vf}/k\). We shall say that \((f_i)\) is a topologically flat surjective family if the morphism from \(\coprod_i X_i\) to X induced by the \(f_i\) is surjective and topologically flat; this means that each \(f_i\) is topologically flat and that every point of X belongs to the image of at least one of the \(X_i\).

It follows from 1.3.3 that the topologically flat surjective families define a pretopology on \(\mathbf{Vf}/k\) (IV 4.2.5); the corresponding topology will be called the flat topology on \(\mathbf{Vf}/k\). It is clear that a contravariant functor F from \(\mathbf{Vf}/k\) to \((\mathrm{Ens})\) is a sheaf for the flat topology if and only if F transforms a direct sum into a direct product and the sequence \[ \mathrm{F}Y \xrightarrow{\ \mathrm{F}f\ } \mathrm{F}X \underset{\mathrm{F}(\mathrm{pr}_2)}{\overset{\mathrm{F}(\mathrm{pr}_1)}{\rightrightarrows}} \mathrm{F}(X\times_Y X) \] is exact for every surjective topologically flat morphism \(f : X \to Y\).

Proposition 1.3.1 implies that the flat topology is coarser than the canonical topology. One therefore obtains a functor i from \(\mathbf{Vf}/k\) to the category of sheaves for the flat topology by associating to every formal variety X the functor \(iX : T \mapsto \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathbf{Vf}/k}(T,X)\). Theorem 1.4 implies that, if \(d_0,d_1 : X_1 \rightrightarrows X\) is an equivalence relation such that \(d_1\) is topologically flat, the formation of the quotient commutes with i: \[ i(X)/i(X_1) \simeq i(X/X_1). \]
To conclude these generalities on formal varieties, it remains for us to define briefly étale formal varieties:

A formal variety X over k is said to be étale if the diagonal morphism \(\Delta_X : X \to X\times X\) is a local isomorphism, that is to say if \(\Delta_X\) induces an isomorphism from \(\mathscr{O}_{X\times X,\Delta(x)}\) onto \(\mathscr{O}_{X,x}\) for every point x of X. One sees easily with the help of SGA I that this formulation is equivalent to the two following ones: the formal variety X is topologically flat, and, for every point \(x\in X\), with projection \(s\in\mathrm{Spf}\,k\), \(\mathscr{O}_{X,x}\widehat{\otimes}_k k(s)\) is a finite separable extension of the residue field \(k(s)\) of s; or again, if A denotes the affine algebra of X, the local components (0.1) of \(A\widehat{\otimes}_k(k/\mathfrak{n})\) are finite étale algebras over \(k/\mathfrak{n}\), for every open ideal \(\mathfrak{n}\) of k.

Denote by \(\mathbf{Ve}/k\) the full subcategory of \(\mathbf{Vf}/k\) formed of the étale varieties. The inclusion of \(\mathbf{Ve}/k\) into \(\mathbf{Vf}/k\) then possesses a left adjoint functor \(X \mapsto X_e\), which can be described in the following way: the variety \(X_e\) has the same points as X; moreover, if x is a point of X, with residue field \(k(x)\) and projection s on \(\mathrm{Spf}\,k\), the local algebra \(\mathscr{O}_{X_e,x}\) is a projective pseudocompact k-module and \(k(s)\widehat{\otimes}_k \mathscr{O}_{X_e,x}\) is isomorphic to the largest separable extension of \(k(s)\) contained in \(k(x)\). For every isomorphism \(q_x\) from \(k(s)\widehat{\otimes}_k \mathscr{O}_{X_e,x}\) into \(k(x)\), there is one and only one morphism of profinite algebras \(p_x : \mathscr{O}_{X_e,x} \to \mathscr{O}_{X,x}\) compatible with \(q_x\) and with the canonical projections of \(\mathscr{O}_{X_e,x}\) and \(\mathscr{O}_{X,x}\) onto \(k(s)\widehat{\otimes}_k \mathscr{O}_{X_e,x}\) and \(k(x)\). These morphisms \(p_x\) define a canonical morphism p from X to \(X_e\), which is functorial in X. It follows from SGA, I that every morphism from X to an étale formal variety factors in one and only one way through p.

Let Y be an étale formal k-variety and \(f : X \to Y\) a morphism of \(\mathbf{Vf}/k\). The graph morphism \(\Gamma_f : X \to X\times Y\), with components \(\mathrm{id}_X\) and f, is then the inverse image of the diagonal morphism \(\Delta_Y : Y \to Y\times Y\) under the base change \(f\times\mathrm{id}_Y : X\times Y \to Y\times Y\). It follows that \(\Gamma_f\) is a local isomorphism, hence that \(f = \mathrm{pr}_2\circ\Gamma_f\) is topologically flat if \(\mathrm{pr}_2\) is, for example if X is topologically flat over k. Conversely, as Y is topologically flat, X is topologically flat over k if X is so over Y (1.3.3). Taking in particular for f the canonical morphism \(p : X \to X_e\) of 1.6, we see that X is topologically flat over \(X_e\) if and only if it is so over k.
2. Generalities on formal groups

Let k be a pseudocompact ring and G a formal k-group, that is to say a group of the category \(\mathbf{Vf}/k\) of formal varieties over k. If A is the affine algebra of G, the composition law of G obviously defines a diagonal morphism, that is to say a homomorphism of profinite k-algebras \(\Delta_A : A \to A\widehat{\otimes}_k A\); this homomorphism satisfies the following conditions:

(i) the diagram \[ A \xrightarrow{\ \Delta_A\ } A\widehat{\otimes}_k A \underset{A\otimes\Delta_A}{\overset{\Delta_A\otimes A}{\rightrightarrows}} A\widehat{\otimes}_k A\widehat{\otimes}_k A \] is commutative.

(ii) there exists an augmentation (necessarily unique), that is to say a homomorphism of profinite k-algebras \(\varepsilon_A : A \to k\) such that the composite maps \[ A \xrightarrow{\ \Delta_A\ } A\widehat{\otimes}_k A \xrightarrow{\ \varepsilon_A\otimes A\ } k\widehat{\otimes}_k A \simeq A \] \[ \text{and}\qquad A \xrightarrow{\ \Delta_A\ } A\widehat{\otimes}_k A \xrightarrow{\ A\otimes\varepsilon_A\ } A\widehat{\otimes}_k k \simeq A \] are the identity maps of A.

(iii) there exists an antipodism (necessarily unique), that is to say a homomorphism of profinite k-algebras \(c_A : A \to A\) such that the composite map \[ A \xrightarrow{\ \Delta_A\ } A\widehat{\otimes}_k A \xrightarrow{\ c_A\otimes A\ } A\widehat{\otimes}_k A \xrightarrow{\ m_A\ } A \] is equal to \(\eta_A\circ\varepsilon_A\), where \(m_A\) denotes the linear map sending \(a\widehat{\otimes}b\) to \(a\cdot b\) and \(\eta_A\) the map \(\lambda \mapsto \lambda 1_A\) from k to A.
It is clear that, conversely, every diagonal morphism \(\Delta_A\) satisfying (i), (ii) and (iii) induces a morphism from \(G\times G\) to G, which makes G into a formal group over k.

In what follows, we shall call the ideal \(I_A = \varepsilon_A^{-1}(0)\) the augmentation ideal of A. We shall denote by \(\omega_{G/k}\) the pseudocompact k-module \(I_A/\overline{I_A^2}\), that is to say the quotient of \(I_A\) by the closed ideal generated by the products \(x\cdot y\), \(x,y\in I_A\).

Let \(\mathbf{H}\) be a group of the category of Coalgebras over \(\mathbf{O}_k\) (1.2.2). For every k-algebra \(C\in\mathrm{Alf}/k\), \(\mathbf{H}(C)\) is thus equipped with a structure of group C-coalgebra (VIIA 3.2; following Manin, we shall say bialgebra instead of group coalgebra); moreover, if \(\varphi : C \to D\) is a morphism of \(\mathrm{Alf}/k\), the map \(d\otimes x \mapsto d\cdot(\mathbf{H}(\varphi))(x)\) from \(D\otimes_C \mathbf{H}(C)\) to \(\mathbf{H}(D)\) is a homomorphism of bialgebras over D. We shall summarize these properties by saying that \(\mathbf{H}\) is a bialgebra over \(\mathbf{O}_k\).

It is clear that the functor \(\mathbf{H} \mapsto \mathrm{Spf}^*\mathbf{H}\) of 1.3.5 commutes with finite products. It therefore transforms a bialgebra over \(\mathbf{O}_k\) into a group-valued k-functor, that is to say into a functor from \(\mathrm{Alf}/k\) to the category of groups; and indeed, for every k-algebra of finite length C, the elements x of \(\mathbf{H}(C)\) belonging to \((\mathrm{Spf}^*\mathbf{H})(C)\) form a group under the multiplication of the algebra \(\mathbf{H}(C)\) (confer VIIA 3.2.2): recall that these elements x are such that \(\varepsilon_{\mathbf{H}(C)}(x) = 1\) and \(\Delta_{\mathbf{H}(C)}(x) = x\otimes x\). The second condition moreover implies the equality \(\varepsilon_{\mathbf{H}(C)}(x) = \varepsilon_{\mathbf{H}(C)}(x)\varepsilon_{\mathbf{H}(C)}(x)\), hence also \(\varepsilon_{\mathbf{H}(C)}(x) = 1\) if k is local.

A bialgebra \(\mathbf{H}\) over \(\mathbf{O}_k\) is said to be flat if the underlying module is flat (1.2.1). If \(\mathbf{H}\) is flat, it is clear that the k-functor \(\mathrm{Spf}^*\mathbf{H}\) is left exact. It is therefore isomorphic to \(C \mapsto \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathbf{Vf}/k}(\mathrm{Spf}\,C,\mathscr{G}(\mathbf{H}))\) for a certain formal group \(\mathscr{G}(\mathbf{H})\); it follows from VIIA 3.2.2 and 1.3.5 that one can describe \(\mathscr{G}(\mathbf{H})\) in the following manner: let \(H^*\) be the pseudocompact k-module dual to \(\mathbf{H}\) (1.2.3). The diagonal morphisms of the hyperalgebras \(\mathbf{H}(C)\) induce a multiplication on \(H^*\) which makes \(H^*\) into a profinite k-algebra. The multiplication in the hyperalgebras \(\mathbf{H}(C)\) induces in \(H^*\) a diagonal morphism satisfying conditions (i), (ii) and (iii) of 2.1. It remains to set \[ \mathscr{G}(\mathbf{H}) = \mathrm{Spf}\,H^*. \]

Conversely, let G be a topologically flat formal k-group with affine algebra A and set \(\mathbf{H}(G) = \mathbf{V}_k^f(A)\) (1.2.3). The diagonal morphism \(\Delta_A : A \to A\widehat{\otimes}_k A\) then induces, for every k-algebra of finite length C, a linear map from \(\mathbf{V}_k^f(A)(C)\otimes_C \mathbf{V}_k^f(A)(C)\) to \(\mathbf{V}_k^f(A)(C)\) which makes the coalgebra \(\mathbf{V}_k^f(A)(C)\) into a bialgebra over C. We say that \(\mathbf{H}(G)\) is the bialgebra of the formal group G. By 1.3.5, the functor \(\mathbf{H} : G \mapsto \mathbf{H}(G)\) is an equivalence from the category of topologically flat formal k-groups to that of flat bialgebras over \(\mathbf{O}_k\). The functor \(\mathscr{G}\) is an "inverse up to functorial isomorphism" of \(\mathbf{H}\).

When k is an artinian ring and G a topologically flat formal k-group, the functor \(\mathbf{H}(G)\) is obviously determined by its value \(H(G) = \mathbf{H}(G)(k)\) at k. One also says that \(H(G)\) is the bialgebra of G. The functor \(H : G \mapsto H(G)\) is then an equivalence from the category of topologically flat formal k-groups to the category of flat bialgebras over k.

Suppose for simplicity that k is artinian and let G be a commutative, topologically flat formal k-group. In this case, the bialgebra \(H(G)\) has a commutative multiplication, so that \(H(G)\) is a co-group in the category of commutative k-algebras (VIIA 3.3).

If k is artinian, one thus sees that the functor \(G \mapsto \mathrm{Spec}\,H(G)\) is an antiequivalence from the category of commutative topologically flat formal k-groups to that of commutative group schemes which are affine and flat over k.
When k is a field, one thus obtains an antiequivalence from the category of commutative topologically flat formal k-groups to that of affine commutative k-group schemes.

Let us now consider an arbitrary formal k-group G, with affine algebra A. Denote as always by \(\mathbf{H}(G)\) the \(\mathbf{O}_k\)-module \(\mathbf{V}_k^f(A)\) dual to A and denote by \(\varphi_G\) the functorial homomorphism \[ \varphi_G : \mathbf{H}(G)\otimes_k \mathbf{H}(G) \longrightarrow \mathbf{H}(G\times G) \] induced by the natural map (0.3.6), for every object C of \(\mathrm{Alf}/k\): \[ (A\widehat{\otimes}_k C)^*\otimes_C (A\widehat{\otimes}_k C)^* \longrightarrow ((A\widehat{\otimes}_k C)\widehat{\otimes}_C (A\widehat{\otimes}_k C))^*. \]

If \(m : G\times G \to G\) is the structural morphism of G, the composite map: \[ \mathbf{H}(G)\otimes_k \mathbf{H}(G) \xrightarrow{\ \varphi_G\ } \mathbf{H}(G\times G) \xrightarrow{\ \mathbf{H}(m)\ } \mathbf{H}(G) \] makes \(\mathbf{H}(G)\) into an algebra over \(\mathbf{O}_k\); for every \(C\in\mathrm{Alf}/k\), the identity element of \(\mathbf{H}(G)(C) = (A\widehat{\otimes}_k C)^*\) is the augmentation of \(A\widehat{\otimes}_k C\) (cf. 2.1). We shall say that \(\mathbf{H}(G)\) is the algebra of the group G.

When G is topologically flat over k, \(\varphi_G\) is an isomorphism and the algebra structure we have just defined obviously coincides with that of 2.2.1. In the general case, however, \(\varphi_G\) is not invertible, so that the morphism \(\delta_G : \mathbf{H}(G) \to \mathbf{H}(G\times G)\), induced by the diagonal morphism "\(x\mapsto(x,x)\)" from G to \(G\times G\), does not factor canonically through \(\mathbf{H}(G)\otimes_k \mathbf{H}(G)\).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 2.3.1

Let K and G be two formal k-groups, K being topologically flat over k. There then exists a canonical bijection from the set \(\mathrm{Hom}_{k\text{-Gr.}}(K,G)\) of homomorphisms of formal k-groups from K to G onto the set of homomorphisms of unitary \(\mathbf{O}_k\)-algebras \(h : \mathbf{H}(K) \to \mathbf{H}(G)\) such that the diagram \[ \xymatrix{ \mathbf{H}(K)\otimes_k \mathbf{H}(K) \ar[rr]^-{h\otimes h} & & \mathbf{H}(G)\otimes_k \mathbf{H}(G) \ar[dr]^-{\varphi_G} & \\ \mathbf{H}(K) \ar[u]^-{\Delta_{\mathbf{H}(K)}} \ar[rr]_-{h} & & \mathbf{H}(G) \ar[r]_-{\delta_G} & \mathbf{H}(G\times G) } \] is commutative.

As K is topologically flat, \(\mathbf{H}(K)\) is equipped with a structure of bialgebra (cf. 2.2) and \(\Delta_{\mathbf{H}(K)}\) is its diagonal morphism; in other words, we have \(\Delta_{\mathbf{H}(K)} = \varphi_K^{-1}\circ \delta_K\). When G is also topologically flat over k, our proposition follows from the equivalence of categories defined in 2.2.1.

In the general case, we may assume k artinian and argue on the algebras H(K) and \(H(G) = \mathbf{H}(G)(k)\). Let then B and A be the affine algebras of K and G, \(\mathrm{Hom}_c(A,B)\) the set of continuous k-linear maps from A to B and \(\mathrm{Hom}_k(B^*,A^*)\) the set of k-linear maps from \(B^*\) to \(A^*\). It is known that the canonical map \(f \mapsto f^*\) from \(\mathrm{Hom}_c(A,B)\) to \(\mathrm{Hom}_k(B^*,A^*)\) is bijective when B is a projective pseudocompact k-module. As this map is moreover functorial in A and B, one easily sees that f is compatible with the diagonal morphisms of A and B if and only if \(f^*\) is compatible with the multiplication of \(A^*\) and that of \(B^*\). Likewise, f is compatible with the multiplication of B and that of A if and only if the diagram \[ \xymatrix{ B^*\otimes_k B^* \ar[r]^-{f^*\otimes f^*} \ar[d]_-{\Delta_B} & A^*\otimes_k A^* \ar[r]^-{\mathrm{can.}} & (A\widehat{\otimes}_k A)^* \ar[d]^-{m_A^*} \\ B^* \ar[rr]_-{f^*} & & A^* } \] is commutative (\(m_A\) denotes the linear map \(a\widehat{\otimes}a' \mapsto a\cdot a'\) from \(A\widehat{\otimes}_k A\) to A).

Finally, if f is a homomorphism of unitary algebras and is compatible with the diagonal morphisms, we have \(\varepsilon_B\circ f = \varepsilon_A\), so that \(f^*\) sends the identity element of \(B^*\) to that of \(A^*\). Conversely, if \(f^*\) is a homomorphism of unitary algebras which makes the diagram above commutative, f is compatible with the multiplications and the diagonal morphisms and we have \(\varepsilon_B\circ f = \varepsilon_A\). From this we deduce the equalities \(\varepsilon_B f(1) = 1\), \(f(1)\cdot f(1) = f(1)\) and \(\Delta_B f(1) = f(1)\widehat{\otimes}f(1)\). If \(c_B\) is the antipodism of B, it follows from 2.1 (iii) that \(f(1)\) has an inverse \(c_B f(1)\) in B, hence that \(f(1)\) is the identity element of B.

Let us now suppose, for simplicity, that the ring k is artinian. When G is topologically flat over k, the algebra \(H(G) = \mathbf{H}(G)(k)\) can be characterized by a universal property (CARTIER): let U be a k-algebra (associative, with identity element) and \(U_m\) the group-valued k-functor which associates to every k-algebra of finite length C the multiplicative group of invertible elements of \(U\otimes_k C\); identify G with the group-valued k-functor \(C \mapsto \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathbf{Vf}/k}(\mathrm{Spf}\,C,G)\) and denote by \(\mathrm{Hom}_{k\text{-Gr.}}(G,U_m)\) the set of homomorphisms of group-valued k-functors from G to \(U_m\). We have the

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition

For every formal group G topologically flat over an artinian ring k and for every k-algebra U, there is a canonical isomorphism \[ \mathrm{Hom}_{k\text{-Gr.}}(G,U_m) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathrm{Hom}_{k\text{-Alg.}}(H(G),U) \]

Indeed, let X be a formal variety over k, M a k-module and \(\mathbf{W}(M)\) the k-functor \(C \mapsto M\otimes_k C\); moreover, denote by \(\mathrm{Hom}_k(X,\mathbf{W}(M))\) the set of morphisms of k-functors from X to \(\mathbf{W}(M)\); if A is the affine algebra of X and if \(\mathfrak{l}\) runs over the open ideals of A, we have canonical isomorphisms \[ \mathrm{Hom}_k(X,\mathbf{W}(M)) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \varprojlim_{\mathfrak{l}} \mathrm{Hom}_k(\mathrm{Spf}\,A/\mathfrak{l}, \mathbf{W}(M)) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \varprojlim_{\mathfrak{l}} M\otimes_k (A/\mathfrak{l}) \] and linear maps \[ M\otimes_k (A/\mathfrak{l}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Hom}_k((A/\mathfrak{l})^*,M) \] which send \(u\otimes x\) to the k-linear map \(f \mapsto f(x)\cdot u\). By passage to the projective limit, we thus obtain maps \[ \mathrm{Hom}_k(X,\mathbf{W}(M)) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } M\widehat{\otimes}_k A \xrightarrow{\ \psi_A\ } \mathrm{Hom}_k(A^*,M) \] where \(M\widehat{\otimes}_k A\) denotes the projective limit of the k-modules \(M\otimes_k(A/\mathfrak{l})\). The map \(\psi_A\) obviously involves only the pseudocompact k-module structure of A; moreover, this map is bijective when A is equal to k, hence more generally when A is a projective pseudocompact k-module (the functor \(N \mapsto M\widehat{\otimes}_k N\) commutes with infinite products).

Suppose now that X equals G and M equals U. For every k-algebra of finite length C, the multiplication then makes \(U\otimes_k C\) into a monoid with identity element; moreover, it is clear that \(\mathrm{Hom}_{k\text{-Gr.}}(G,U_m)\) is the subset of \(\mathrm{Hom}_k(G,\mathbf{W}(U))\) formed of the homomorphisms of k-functors valued in monoids with identity element. When A is a projective pseudocompact k-module, it follows from the functorial character of \(\psi_A\) that these homomorphisms correspond to the k-linear maps from A to U which are compatible with the multiplication and with the identity elements of A and U.
Let us now return to an arbitrary pseudocompact ring k in order to apply to formal groups the results of § 1 on passage to the quotient by a topologically flat equivalence relation.

If \(u : F \to G\) is a monomorphism of formal k-groups, \(\mu : G\times G \to G\) the "multiplication" morphism of G and \(\lambda\) the composite morphism \[ \lambda : F\times G \xrightarrow{\ u\times\mathrm{id}_G\ } G\times G \xrightarrow{\ \mu\ } G, \] it is known indeed that the pair \[ F\times G \underset{\lambda}{\overset{\mathrm{pr}_2}{\rightrightarrows}} G \] is an equivalence pair (cf. V, 2.a and VIA, 3.1). If F is topologically flat over k, \(\mathrm{pr}_2\) is topologically flat and we may apply theorem 1.4: the canonical projection p of G onto the variety \(F\backslash G\) of left cosets of G modulo F is therefore topologically flat; on the other hand, the morphism \(F\times G \to G\times_{F\backslash G} G\) with components \(\lambda\) and \(\mathrm{pr}_2\) is invertible; when T is a formal variety of finite length over k and x, y two elements of \(G(T)\), x and y therefore have the same image in \((F\backslash G)(T)\) if and only if there is a z in \(F(T)\) such that \(y = u(z)\cdot x\). Taking for x the zero morphism, which factors through the unit section of G, we see that y has the same image in \((F\backslash G)(T)\) as the identity element of \(G(T)\) if and only if y belongs to the image of \(F(T)\) under \(u(T)\).

In particular, if u is an isomorphism from F onto a normal subgroup of G, the structural morphism from \(G\times G\) to G manifestly induces, by passage to the quotient, a structure of formal group on \(F\backslash G\); what precedes then shows that u is an isomorphism from F onto \(\mathrm{Ker}\,p\).

Let \(f : G \to H\) be a homomorphism of formal k-groups, F the kernel of f and u the inclusion of F in G. If F is topologically flat over k, the homomorphism \(f' : F\backslash G \to H\) induced by f is a monomorphism; this is a consequence of the results of exposé IV; we nevertheless give a direct proof of it: let T be a formal variety of finite length over k and t an element of \((F\backslash G)(T)\) such that \(f'\circ t\) is the identity element of \(H(T)\). We must show that t is the identity element of \((F\backslash G)(T)\); as the canonical projection \(\mathrm{pr}_1\) of \(T\times_{F\backslash G} G\) onto T is surjective and topologically flat (2.4), it suffices to show (1.3.1) that \(t\circ\mathrm{pr}_1\) is the identity element of \((F\backslash G)(T\times_{F\backslash G} G)\).
