As we have \(t\circ\mathrm{pr}_1 = p\circ\mathrm{pr}_2\), where \(\mathrm{pr}_2\) is the canonical projection of \(T\times_{F\backslash G} G\) onto G, the equality \(1 = f'\circ(p\circ\mathrm{pr}_2) = f\circ\mathrm{pr}_2\) and the exact sequence \[ 1 \longrightarrow F \longrightarrow G \longrightarrow H \] show that \(\mathrm{pr}_2\) factors through F, hence that \(p\circ\mathrm{pr}_2\) is zero.

If B, C and D are the affine algebras of G, H and \(F\backslash G\), then \(p : G \to F\backslash G\) induces an injection from D into B and \(f'\) induces a surjection from C onto D (1.3). Consequently, D is the image of C in B.

Suppose now that F and G are topologically flat over k: as G is topologically flat over k and over \(F\backslash G\), \(F\backslash G\) is topologically flat over k (1.3.3); it is clear on the other hand that we have \(\mathrm{Coker}\,f = \mathrm{Coker}\,f'\); as \(f'\) is a monomorphism, the canonical projection of H onto \(\mathrm{Coker}\,f'\) is topologically flat and \(f'\) is an isomorphism from \(F\backslash G\) onto \(\mathrm{Ker}\,q\); if \(\mathrm{Ker}\,f\) and G are topologically flat over k, we thus see that we have the equalities \(\mathrm{Coim}\,f = \mathrm{Im}\,f\); whence

blocks.title: Théorème
blocks.html:
Theorem

Commutative formal groups over a field form an abelian category.

blocks.title: Corollaire
blocks.html:
Corollary

Affine commutative group schemes over a field form an abelian category.
This follows from 2.2.2.

A formal k-group is said to be étale if the underlying formal variety is étale (1.6); these formal groups have a very simple structure: indeed, suppose k local; let \(\overline{k}\) be the residue field of k, \(k_s\) a separable closure of \(\overline{k}\) and \(\Gamma\) the topological Galois group of \(k_s\) over \(\overline{k}\); call a \(\Gamma\)-set the data of a set E and of a continuous action of \(\Gamma\) on E (the isotropy group of an element \(x\in E\) is thus an open subgroup of \(\Gamma\)). Finally, associate to every formal variety X over k the inductive limit \(X(k_s)\) of the sets \(X(\ell)\), \(\ell\) running over the finite extensions of \(\overline{k}\) contained in \(k_s\). It is clear that \(\Gamma\) acts continuously on \(X(k_s)\) and it follows from SGA 1, I 8.3, that the functor \(X \mapsto X(k_s)\) is an equivalence from the category of étale formal varieties over k to that of \(\Gamma\)-sets.

This functor \(X \mapsto X(k_s)\) therefore induces an equivalence from the category of étale formal groups over k (local) to that of \(\Gamma\)-groups, that is to say the groups of the category of \(\Gamma\)-sets (such a \(\Gamma\)-group thus consists of the data of an abstract group G and of a continuous action of \(\Gamma\) on G by group automorphisms).

Suppose again that the pseudocompact ring k is arbitrary. It is clear that the functor \(G \mapsto G_e\) of 1.6.1 commutes with finite products. It therefore transforms a formal group into an étale formal group. Moreover, as the morphism \(p : G \to G_e\) of 1.6 is functorial in G, it is a homomorphism of formal groups when G is equipped with a group structure; consider the kernel of this homomorphism; as p induces a bijection on the underlying sets, \(\mathrm{Ker}\,p\) has for underlying set the image of \(\mathrm{Spf}\,k\) under the unit section; if we identify the sets underlying \(\mathrm{Spf}\,k\) and \(\mathrm{Ker}\,p\) by means of this unit section, \(G_e\) has the same local algebra as \(\mathrm{Spf}\,k\) at a point g of \(\mathrm{Ker}\,p\); consequently, \(\mathrm{Ker}\,p\) has for local algebra at g the tensor product \(k\widehat{\otimes}_k \mathscr{O}_{G,g}\), that is to say \(\mathscr{O}_{G,g}\). For these reasons we shall say that \(\mathrm{Ker}\,p\) is the infinitesimal neighborhood of the origin of G and we shall write \(\mathrm{Ker}\,p = G_0\), thus obtaining an exact sequence \[ 1 \longrightarrow G_0 \xrightarrow{\ \mathrm{incl.}\ } G \xrightarrow{\ p\ } G_e. \]
In what follows, we shall say that G is infinitesimal if \(G = G_0\).

When G is a formal group topologically flat over k, \(p : G \to G_e\) is surjective and topologically flat by 1.6.1, so that \(G_e\) is identified with the quotient \(G/G_0\) and we have an exact sequence \[ 1 \longrightarrow G_0 \xrightarrow{\ \mathrm{incl.}\ } G \xrightarrow{\ p\ } G_e \longrightarrow 1. \]

In the case where k is a perfect field, the functor \(X \mapsto X_e\) of 1.6 is also right adjoint to the inclusion of \(\mathbf{Ve}/k\) into \(\mathbf{Vf}/k\): precisely, if k is a perfect field, \(X_e\) has the same points as X and has for local algebra at a point x the residue field of \(\mathscr{O}_{X,x}\). The canonical projections of the algebras \(\mathscr{O}_{X,x}\) onto their residue fields therefore define a section \(s : X_e \to X\) of p. This section depends functorially on X; it is therefore a homomorphism of formal groups when X is a formal group.

When k is a perfect field, one thus sees that a formal group G over k is canonically isomorphic to the semi-direct product of an infinitesimal group \(G_0\) and an étale group \(G_e\) acting on \(G_0\).

If, moreover, we assume G commutative, G is the product of \(G_0\) and \(G_e\). This decomposition of commutative formal groups over k corresponds to an analogous decomposition of affine commutative k-group schemes (2.2.2): let us say that a commutative group scheme over a field k is of multiplicative type (resp. unipotent) if it is isomorphic to \(\mathrm{Spec}\,H(G)\), where G is an étale (resp. infinitesimal) commutative formal k-group. By 2.5.1, every affine commutative group scheme G over a field k contains a subgroup of multiplicative type \(G_m\) such that \(G/G_m\) is unipotent. When k is perfect, 2.5.1 moreover implies the existence of a canonical retraction of G onto \(G_m\), so that G is the product of a unipotent group and a group of multiplicative type.
We shall now study infinitesimal formal groups, to which the following sections are devoted. In this study, Lie algebras play a primordial role.
Suppose first that the base ring k is artinian and let G be a formal group over k. One can give three different interpretations of the Lie algebra of G, all of which we shall use:

a) Let D be the algebra \(k[d]/(d^2)\) of dual numbers over k and \(\delta\) the homomorphism from D to k which annihilates d. For every formal group G over k, \(\mathrm{Lie}(G)\) is the kernel of \(G(\delta)\), so that by definition we have an exact sequence of groups \[ 0 \longrightarrow \mathrm{Lie}(G) \longrightarrow G(D) \xrightarrow{\ G(\delta)\ } G(k) \longrightarrow 1. \]

b) If G has A for affine algebra, the group \(G(D)\) has for elements the homomorphisms of profinite algebras \(f : A \to D\). The condition \(G(\delta)(f)=1\) is equivalent to \(\delta\circ f = \varepsilon_A\). For every \(x\in A\), we therefore have the relation \[ f(x) = \varepsilon_A(x)\cdot 1_D + f'(y)\cdot d, \] where \(f'\) is a continuous linear map from \(I_A/\overline{I_A^2}\) to k and where y is the class of \(x-\varepsilon_A(x)1_A\) modulo \(\overline{I_A^2}\). It is clear that the map \(f\mapsto f'\) is a bijection from Lie G onto the topological dual of \(\omega_{G/k}\) (0.2.2 and 2.1). This bijection obviously respects the group structures: indeed, let f and g be two elements of Lie G. Their product is the composite map \(h\circ\Delta_A\), where \(h : A\widehat{\otimes}A \to D\) is such that \(h(a\widehat{\otimes}b) = f(a)\cdot g(b)\). But it follows from 2.1 (ii) that we have \(\Delta_A a - a\widehat{\otimes}1 - 1\widehat{\otimes}a \in I_A\widehat{\otimes}I_A\) when \(a\in I_A\); if \(a\in I_A\), we consequently have \((f\cdot g)(a) = f(a)+g(a)\) (confer also II 3/10).

In what follows, we identify \(\mathrm{Lie}(G)\) with \(\omega_{G/k}^*\) by means of the bijection \(f\mapsto f'\) described above. The group \(\mathrm{Lie}(G)\) is thus equipped with a structure of k-module.

c) Let \(A^*\) and \(D^*\) be the k-modules dual to A and D, \(\{1_D^*,d^*\}\) the basis dual to the basis \(\{1_D,d\}\) of D over k (\(1_D^* = \delta\)). As D is free of finite rank over k, the canonical map \(f\mapsto f^*\) from the set \(\mathrm{Hom}_c(A,D)\) of morphisms of pseudocompact k-modules from A to D into \(\mathrm{Hom}_k(D^*,A^*)\) is bijective. On the other hand, \(f^*\) is determined by the values \(f^*(1_D^*)\) and \(f^*(d^*) = x\). The condition \(G(\delta)(f) = 1\) is equivalent to the equality \(f^*(1_D^*) = \varepsilon_A\). One sees easily, on the other hand, that f is compatible with the multiplication if and only if we have \[ (*)\qquad \delta_G x = \varphi_G(x\otimes 1 + 1\otimes x) \] (cf. 2.3).

Finally, it is clear that a continuous linear map \(f : A \to D\) which is compatible with the multiplication and such that \(\delta\circ f = \varepsilon_A\) sends identity element to identity element. The map \(f\mapsto x\) therefore allows us to identify Lie G with the subset of \(H(G)\) formed of the elements satisfying relation \((*)\). If x and y are two elements of \(H(G)\) satisfying \((*)\), we have \[ \begin{aligned} \delta_G(xy) &= (\delta_G x)(\delta_G y) = \varphi_G((x\otimes 1+1\otimes x)(y\otimes 1+1\otimes y))\\ &= \varphi_G(xy\otimes 1+x\otimes y+y\otimes x+1\otimes xy), \end{aligned} \] whence \(\delta_G(xy-yx) = \varphi_G((xy-yx)\otimes 1+1\otimes(xy-yx))\).
This shows that the k-module Lie G is identified with a Lie subalgebra of \(H(G)\): we shall say that Lie G is the Lie algebra of G.

When k is an arbitrary pseudocompact ring and G a formal group over k, we call the \(\mathbf{O}_k\)-Lie algebra of G the functor \(\mathbf{Lie}(G)\) which associates to every profinite k-algebra of finite length C the Lie algebra \(\mathrm{Lie}(G\widehat{\otimes}_k C)\) of \(G\widehat{\otimes}_k C\) over C (1.2.3); this Lie algebra is flat over \(\mathbf{O}_k\) when \(\omega_{G/k}\) is a projective pseudocompact k-module.

Conversely, every Lie algebra \(\mathbf{L}\) over \(\mathbf{O}_k\) defines a group-valued k-functor: indeed, denote by \(\mathbf{U}(\mathbf{L})\) the functor which associates to every k-algebra of finite length C the enveloping algebra \(U(\mathbf{L}(C))\) of the Lie algebra \(\mathbf{L}(C)\) over C. By VIIA 3.2.2, \(\mathbf{U}(\mathbf{L})\) is a bialgebra over \(\mathbf{O}_k\) and determines a group-valued k-functor \(\mathrm{Spf}^*\mathbf{U}(\mathbf{L})\) (2.2) which we shall from now on denote \(\mathscr{G}(\mathbf{L})\): \(\mathscr{G}(\mathbf{L})(C)\) is thus the group of elements \(z\in U(\mathbf{L}(C))\) of augmentation 1 and such that \(\Delta_{U(\mathbf{L}(C))}(z) = z\otimes z\).

When \(\mathbf{L}\) is a flat Lie algebra over \(\mathbf{O}_k\), the bialgebra \(\mathbf{U}(\mathbf{L})\) is flat over \(\mathbf{O}_k\) by the Poincaré-Birkhoff-Witt theorem (Bourbaki, Alg. de Lie, I 2.7); consequently, \(\mathscr{G}(\mathbf{L})\) is a formal group topologically flat over k which has \(\mathbf{U}(\mathbf{L})\) as its \(\mathbf{O}_k\)-bialgebra. This group is radicial: indeed, one reduces directly to the case where k is artinian (2.6). Set then \(U = U(\mathbf{L})\) and let \(U^+\) be the two-sided ideal of U generated by the image of \(\mathbf{L}\). Set moreover \[ \begin{aligned} U_0 &= k\cdot 1_U\\ U_n &= \{u\in U \mid \Delta_U u - u\otimes 1 \in U_{n-1}\otimes U^+\}\\ \text{and}\qquad U_n^+ &= U_n\cap U^+. \end{aligned} \]

By 1.3.6, it suffices to show that U is the union of the \(U_n\). Now, if we identify \(\mathbf{L}\) with its canonical image in U, \(\mathbf{L}\) is obviously contained in \(U_1\). If \(x_1,\ldots,x_n\) are elements of \(\mathbf{L}\), we therefore have \(\Delta_U(x_1\cdots x_n) = (x_1\otimes 1+1\otimes x_1)\cdots(x_n\otimes 1+1\otimes x_n)\), which shows, by induction on n, that the product \(x_1\cdots x_n\) belongs to \(U_n\), hence that \(U = \bigcup_n U_n\).

If \(\mathbf{L}\) is a flat Lie algebra over \(\mathbf{O}_k\), the formal group \(\mathscr{G}(\mathbf{L})\) can be characterized by a universal property: indeed, consider a homomorphism h from \(\mathscr{G}(\mathbf{L})\) into a formal group G; such a homomorphism induces a homomorphism \(\mathbf{Lie}\,h\) from the Lie algebra of \(\mathscr{G}(\mathbf{L})\) into that of G. Composing \(\mathbf{Lie}\,h\) with the canonical monomorphism from \(\mathbf{L}\) into \(\mathbf{U}(\mathbf{L})\), one obtains a homomorphism \(h' : \mathbf{L} \to \mathrm{Lie}\,G\).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition

If G is a formal k-group and \(\mathbf{L}\) a flat Lie algebra over \(\mathbf{O}_k\), the map \(h\mapsto h'\) defined above is a bijection \[ \mathrm{Hom}_{k\text{-Gr.}}(\mathscr{G}(\mathbf{L}),G) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathrm{Hom}(\mathbf{L},\mathrm{Lie}\,G) \] from the set of homomorphisms of formal k-groups from \(\mathscr{G}(\mathbf{L})\) to G onto that of morphisms of \(\mathbf{O}_k\)-Lie algebras from \(\mathbf{L}\) to \(\mathrm{Lie}\,G\).

Indeed, one reduces at once to the case where k is artinian. In this case, \(\mathrm{Hom}_{k\text{-Gr.}}(\mathscr{G}(\mathbf{L}),G)\) is in canonical one-to-one correspondence (2.3.1) with the set of homomorphisms of unitary algebras \(g : U(\mathbf{L}) \to H(G)\) such that the following diagram is commutative \[ \xymatrix{ \mathbf{U}(\mathbf{L}) \ar[rr]^-{g} \ar[d]_-{\Delta_{\mathbf{U}(\mathbf{L})}} & & \mathbf{H}(G) \ar[dr]^-{\delta_G} & \\ \mathbf{U}(\mathbf{L})\otimes\mathbf{U}(\mathbf{L}) \ar[rr]_-{g\otimes g} & & \mathbf{H}(G)\otimes\mathbf{H}(G) \ar[r]_-{\varphi_G} & \mathbf{H}(G\times G) } \] Now g is defined by its restriction to \(\mathbf{L}\), which is a homomorphism of Lie algebras from \(\mathbf{L}\) into the Lie algebra underlying \(H(G)\). The commutativity of the diagram therefore means simply that g maps \(\mathbf{L}\) into the subset Lie G of \(H(G)\) formed of the x such that \(\delta_G x = \varphi_G(x\otimes 1+1\otimes x)\).

We conclude these generalities with a statement which goes back to S. Lie and which will serve us in section 5. A formal monoid with identity element over k is by definition a pair \((M,m)\) formed of a formal variety M and a morphism \(m : M\times M \to M\) such that \(m(C)\) makes \(M(C)\) into a monoid with identity element for every object C of \(\mathrm{Alf}/k\) (0.4.2). In particular, the identity element of \(M(k)\) defines a section \(\varepsilon_M\) of the canonical projection of M onto \(\mathrm{Spf}\,k\). We shall say that the formal monoid M is infinitesimal if \(\varepsilon_M\) induces a bijection of the underlying sets.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition

Every topologically flat and infinitesimal formal k-monoid M is a formal group.

We must show that \(M(C)\) is a group for every object C of \(\mathrm{Alf}/k\). One thus reduces at once to the case where k is artinian. Let then \(U = H(M)\) be the coalgebra of M (1.3.5); the structural morphism m induces a homomorphism of coalgebras \(m_U\) from \(U\otimes U\) to U, which makes U into an associative algebra over k; this algebra has for identity element the image of the identity element of k under the map from k to U induced by the unit section \(\varepsilon_M\) of M. Likewise, the canonical projection of M onto \(\mathrm{Spf}\,k\) induces a homomorphism \(\varepsilon_U\) from U to k; we shall denote by \(U^+\) the kernel of \(\varepsilon_U\).

We must show that there exists an antipodism, that is to say a homomorphism of coalgebras \(c_U : U \to U\) such that the composite map \[ U \xrightarrow{\ \Delta_U\ } U\otimes_k U \xrightarrow{\ c_U\otimes U\ } U\otimes_k U \xrightarrow{\ m_U\ } U \] sends \(u\in U\) to \(\varepsilon_U(u)\cdot 1_U\). As M is infinitesimal, \(U^+\) is the union of submodules \(U_n^+\) defined as in 2.6.2; one then shows easily, by induction on n, that there exists one and only one linear map \(c_n : U_n^+ \to U_n\) such that the composite map \[ U_n^+ \xrightarrow{\ \mathrm{incl.}\ } U \xrightarrow{\ \Delta_U\ } U\otimes U \xrightarrow{\ c_U\otimes U\ } U\otimes U \xrightarrow{\ m_U\ } U \] is zero. In particular, \(c_{n+1}\) extends \(c_n\) and we may set \[ c_U(x) = \varepsilon_U(x)\cdot 1_U + c_n(x-\varepsilon_U(x)\cdot 1_U), \] where n is large enough for \(U_n^+\) to contain \(x-\varepsilon_U(x)\cdot 1_U\).
3. Phenomena particular to characteristic 0
Throughout this section 3, we assume that the pseudocompact ring k contains the field of rational numbers \(\mathbb{Q}\).

blocks.title: Lemme
blocks.html:
Lemma 3.1

Let C be a commutative \(\mathbb{Q}\)-algebra with element 1, \(\mathbf{L}\) a Lie algebra over C whose underlying C-module is free. The canonical map from \(\mathbf{L}\) into the enveloping algebra \(U(\mathbf{L})\) is an isomorphism from \(\mathbf{L}\) onto the set of primitive elements of \(U(\mathbf{L})\).

Indeed, identify \(\mathbf{L}\) with its canonical image in \(U(\mathbf{L})\); let I be a totally ordered set and \((x_i)_{i\in I}\) a basis of \(\mathbf{L}\) indexed by I; denote by \(\mathbb{N}^{(I)}\) the set of families \(n=(n_i)_{i\in I}\) of natural integers such that \(n_i\) is zero except perhaps for a finite number of indices \(i_1<i_2<\cdots<i_s\) (these indices depend on n); finally set \(x^n = x_{i_1}^{n_{i_1}} x_{i_2}^{n_{i_2}} \cdots x_{i_s}^{n_{i_s}}\) and \(n! = (n_{i_1}!)(n_{i_2}!)\cdots(n_{i_s}!)\).

It is then known that the \(x^n\) form a basis of \(U(\mathbf{L})\) (Poincaré-Birkhoff-Witt theorem) and one sees easily that we have \[ (*)\qquad \Delta_{U(\mathbf{L})}\left(\frac{x^n}{n!}\right) = \sum \frac{x^m}{m!}\otimes\frac{x^{n-m}}{(n-m)!} \] the sum being extended over all elements m of \(\mathbb{N}^{(I)}\) such that \(0\leqslant m\leqslant n\) (i.e. such that \(0\leqslant m_i\leqslant n_i\ \forall i\)). It obviously follows that we have \(\Delta_{U(\mathbf{L})}(u) = u\otimes 1+1\otimes u\) if and only if u is a linear combination of the \(x_i\).

Suppose now that C is artinian, with radical \(\mathfrak{r}\). For every C-algebra U (associative, unitary), the product \(\mathfrak{r}U\) is thus formed of nilpotent elements; if x belongs to \(\mathfrak{r}U\), we shall set \[ \exp_U x = 1+x+\frac{x^2}{2!}+\cdots\ . \]

One thus obtains a bijection from \(\mathfrak{r}U\) onto \(1+\mathfrak{r}U\); the inverse bijection sends an element \(1+y\) of \(1+\mathfrak{r}U\) to \[ \log_U(1+y) = y - \frac{y^2}{2} + \frac{y^3}{3} - \cdots\ . \]
Moreover, it is clear that the map \(\exp_U\) is functorial in U.

The ring C still being artinian, suppose U equipped with a structure of bialgebra over C (cf. 2.2). For every primitive element x of \(\mathfrak{r}U\) (cf. VIIA 3.2.3), we then have \[ \begin{aligned} \Delta_U(\exp_U x) &= \exp_{U\otimes U}(\Delta_U(x))\\ &= \exp_{U\otimes U}(x\otimes 1+1\otimes x)\\ &= \exp_{U\otimes U}(x\otimes 1)\cdot\exp_{U\otimes U}(1\otimes x)\\ &= \big((\exp_U x)\otimes 1\big)\cdot\big(1\otimes(\exp_U x)\big)\\ &= (\exp_U x)\otimes(\exp_U x). \end{aligned} \]

One thus sees that the bijection \(\exp_U\) transforms a primitive element of \(\mathfrak{r}U\) into an element z of \(1+\mathfrak{r}U\) such that \(\Delta_U(z) = z\otimes z\), and the converse is clear.

Consider in particular a Lie algebra \(\mathbf{L}\) flat over C, take for U the enveloping algebra \(U(\mathbf{L})\) of \(\mathbf{L}\) over C and identify \(\mathbf{L}\) with its canonical image in U. By lemma 3.1, \(\mathbf{L}\) is thus the set of primitive elements of U (indeed \(\mathbf{L}\) is a product of free modules over the local components of C). Consider on the other hand the quotient \(\overline{C} = C/\mathfrak{m}\) of C by a maximal ideal \(\mathfrak{m}\). As the formal \(\overline{C}\)-group \(\mathscr{G}(\mathbf{L}\otimes_C\overline{C})\), which has \(U(\mathbf{L}\otimes_C\overline{C})\) for bialgebra, is infinitesimal (2.6.2), the identity element of \(U(\mathbf{L}\otimes_C\overline{C})\) is the only element \(\overline{z}\) such that \(\Delta_{U(\mathbf{L}\otimes_C\overline{C})}(\overline{z}) = \overline{z}\otimes\overline{z}\). It follows that the elements z of \(U(\mathbf{L})\) such that \(\Delta_U(z) = z\otimes z\) necessarily belong to \(1+\mathfrak{r}U\).

Finally, as \(\mathbf{L}\cap\mathfrak{r}U\) is identified with \(\mathbf{L}\otimes_C \mathfrak{r}\), one finally sees that: \(\exp_U\) defines a bijection from \(\mathbf{L}\otimes_C \mathfrak{r}\) onto the group \(\mathscr{G}(\mathbf{L})(C)\). We summarize:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition

Let k be a pseudocompact ring containing \(\mathbb{Q}\), \(\mathbf{L}\) a Lie algebra flat over \(\mathbf{O}_k\), C a profinite k-algebra of finite length and \(\mathfrak{r}(C)\) the radical of C. The map \[ \exp_{U(\mathbf{L}(C))} : \mathbf{L}(C)\otimes_C \mathfrak{r}(C) \longrightarrow \mathscr{G}(\mathbf{L})(C) \] is bijective and functorial in C and \(\mathbf{L}\).

The bijection \(\exp_{U(\mathbf{L}(C))}\) allows one to define, by transport of structure, a group law on the set \(\mathbf{L}(C)\otimes_C \mathfrak{r}(C)\) (which is identified with a subset of \(U(\mathbf{L}(C))\) as in 3.2). If x and y are two elements of \(\mathbf{L}(C)\otimes_C \mathfrak{r}(C)\), this law is such that \[ \begin{aligned} x\cdot y &= \log\big((\exp x)(\exp y)\big) = \log\Big(1+ \sum_{p+q>0} \frac{x^p}{p!}\frac{y^q}{q!}\Big)\\ &= \sum_{m\geqslant 1}\ \sum_{p_i+q_i>0} \frac{(-1)^{m-1}}{m}\cdot\frac{x^{p_1}}{p_1!}\frac{y^{q_1}}{q_1!}\cdots\frac{x^{p_m}}{p_m!}\frac{y^{q_m}}{q_m!} = \sum_{\ell\geqslant 1} \mathrm{P}_\ell(x,y) \end{aligned} \] where \(\mathrm{P}_\ell(x,y)\) denotes the sum of the monomials of total degree \(\ell\) in x and y. We have for example: \[ \mathrm{P}_1(x,y) = x+y \] \[ \mathrm{P}_2(x,y) = \underbrace{\frac{x^2}{2}+xy+\frac{y^2}{2}}_{m=1} - \underbrace{\frac{1}{2}(x^2+xy+yx+y^2)}_{m=2} = \frac{1}{2}(xy-yx) = \frac{1}{2}[x,y] \] \[ \begin{aligned} \mathrm{P}_3(x,y) &= \underbrace{\frac{x^3}{6}+\frac{x^2y}{2}+\frac{xy^2}{2}+\frac{y^3}{6}}_{m=1}\\ &\quad -\underbrace{\frac{1}{2}\Big(x^3+\frac{3}{2}x^2y+\frac{1}{2}yx^2+xyx+yxy+\frac{1}{2}y^2x+\frac{3}{2}xy^2+y^3\Big)}_{m=2}\\ &\quad +\underbrace{\frac{1}{3}(x^3+x^2y+yx^2+xyx+yxy+y^2x+xy^2+y^3)}_{m=3}\\ &= \frac{1}{12}(x^2y+yx^2-2xyx-2yxy+y^2x+xy^2)\\ &= \frac{1}{12}[[y,x],x] + \frac{1}{12}[y,[y,x]]. \end{aligned} \]

One can show more generally that we have \[ \mathrm{P}_\ell(x,y) = \sum \frac{(-1)^{m+\ell}}{m\cdot\ell}\,(\mathrm{ad}\,y)^{q_m}(\mathrm{ad}\,x)^{p_m}\cdots(\mathrm{ad}\,y)^{q_2}(\mathrm{ad}\,x)^{p_2}(\mathrm{ad}\,y)^{q_1}(\mathrm{ad}\,x)^{p_1-1} x \] where \(p_1,q_1,\ldots,p_m,q_m\) run over the sequences of natural integers such that \(p_i+q_i>0\) and \(\sum(p_i+q_i)=\ell\) (for a proof of this Campbell-Hausdorff formula, see Jacobson, Lie Algebras, V § 5, Inter-science Publishers).

If G is a formal k-group with affine algebra A, recall that we denote by \(I_A\) the augmentation ideal of A and by \(\omega_{G/k}\) the pseudocompact k-module \(I_A/\overline{I_A^2} \simeq I_A\widehat{\otimes}_A(A/I_A)\).

blocks.title: Théorème (Cartier)
blocks.html:
Theorem (Cartier)

Let k be a local pseudocompact ring containing the field \(\mathbb{Q}\) and G a formal group over k. The following assertions are equivalent:

  1. (i)

    There exists a flat \(\mathbf{O}_k\)-Lie algebra \(\mathbf{L}\) such that G is isomorphic to \(\mathscr{G}(\mathbf{L})\).

  2. (ii)

    There exists a projective pseudocompact k-module \(\omega\) such that the formal variety underlying G is isomorphic to the variety \(\mathbf{V}_k^0(\omega)\) with affine algebra \(k[[\omega]]\) (1.2.5).

  3. (iii)

    G is infinitesimal and \(\omega_{G/k}\) is a projective pseudocompact k-module.

  4. (iv)

    G is infinitesimal and topologically flat over k.

\((\mathrm{i})\Rightarrow(\mathrm{ii})\): Let \(\omega\) be the pseudocompact k-module dual to \(\mathbf{L}\) (1.2.3). For every profinite k-algebra of finite length C, we must exhibit an isomorphism from \(\mathbf{V}_k^0(\omega)(C)\) onto \(\mathscr{G}(\mathbf{L})(C)\) which is functorial in C. By 1.2.5, \(\mathbf{V}_k^0(\omega)(C)\) is identified with the set \(\mathrm{Hom}_c(\omega,\mathfrak{r}(C))\) of continuous k-linear maps from \(\omega\) into the radical of C. This set is naturally isomorphic to the set \(\mathrm{Hom}_c(\omega\widehat{\otimes}_kC, \mathfrak{r}(C))\) of continuous C-linear maps from \(\omega\widehat{\otimes}_kC\) to \(\mathfrak{r}(C)\); finally, as \(\omega\widehat{\otimes}_kC\) is a projective pseudocompact C-module, the canonical map \[ (\omega\widehat{\otimes}_kC)^*\otimes_C \mathfrak{r}(C) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Hom}_c(\omega\widehat{\otimes}_kC, \mathfrak{r}(C)) \] is bijective. As \((\omega\widehat{\otimes}_kC)^*\) is identified with \(\mathbf{L}(C)\), one finally sees that \(\mathbf{V}_k^0(\omega)(C)\) is canonically isomorphic to \(\mathbf{L}(C)\otimes_C \mathfrak{r}(C)\). The implication \((\mathrm{i})\Rightarrow(\mathrm{ii})\) therefore follows from proposition 3.2.

\((\mathrm{ii})\Rightarrow(\mathrm{iii})\): Let h be an isomorphism from \(k[[\omega]]\) onto the affine algebra A of G. Composing h with the augmentation \(\varepsilon_A\) (2.1), one obtains a homomorphism \(\varepsilon_A h : k[[\omega]] \to k\), which is determined by the restriction \(\lambda\) of \(\varepsilon_A h\) to \(\omega\); this restriction sends \(\omega\) into the radical \(\mathfrak{r}\) of k and the map \(x\mapsto x-\lambda(x)\) from \(\omega\) into the radical of \(k[[\omega]]\) extends to an endomorphism \(\ell_\lambda\) of \(k[[\omega]]\). The equalities \(\ell_\lambda\circ\ell_{-\lambda} = \ell_{-\lambda}\circ\ell_\lambda = \mathrm{id}_{k[[\omega]]}\) show that \(\ell_\lambda\) is an automorphism of \(k[[\omega]]\). Consequently, \(h\ell_\lambda\) is an isomorphism from \(k[[\omega]]\) onto A and \(\varepsilon_A h\ell_\lambda\) maps \(\omega\) to 0. Replacing h by \(h\ell_\lambda\) if necessary, we may therefore assume that \(\varepsilon_A h\) vanishes on the closed ideal I of \(k[[\omega]]\) generated by \(\omega\): in this case, h induces a bijection from \(I/I^2\) onto \(I_A/\overline{I_A^2}\); as \(I/I^2\) is identified with \(\omega\), one sees that \(\omega_{G/k}\) is projective. It is clear on the other hand that \(\mathrm{Spf}\,\mathbf{V}_k^0(\omega)\) is infinitesimal, as is G.

\((\mathrm{iii})\Rightarrow(\mathrm{i})\): Indeed, let \(\mathbf{L}\) be the \(\mathbf{O}_k\)-Lie algebra of G; this algebra has for underlying \(\mathbf{O}_k\)-module the dual of \(\omega_{G/k}\) (2.1); consequently, \(\mathbf{L}\) is flat over \(\mathbf{O}_k\). If we identify \(k[[\omega_{G/k}]]\) with the affine algebra of the formal group \(\mathscr{G}(\mathbf{L})\) (this is possible by \((\mathrm{i})\Rightarrow(\mathrm{ii})\)), the identity morphism of \(\mathbf{L}\) is canonically associated to a continuous homomorphism of algebras \[ a : A \longrightarrow k[[\omega_{G/k}]]. \]

We must show that a, which by definition induces an isomorphism from \(\omega_{G/k}\) onto \(\omega_{\mathscr{G}(\mathbf{L})/k}\), is an isomorphism: For this, consider a section \(\tau\) of the canonical projection of \(I_A\) onto \(\omega_{G/k}\). Such a section defines, by 1.2.5, a continuous homomorphism of algebras \[ b : k[[\omega_{G/k}]] \longrightarrow A. \]

Let I be the closed ideal of \(k[[\omega_{G/k}]]\) generated by \(\omega_{G/k}\) and filter \(k[[\omega_{G/k}]]\) (resp. A) by the closures of the ideals \(I^n\) (resp. \(I_A^n\)). The composite \(a\tau\) is obviously a section of the canonical projection of I onto \(\omega = I/\overline{I^2}\). Consequently, \(ab\) induces the identity map on \(I/\overline{I^2}\), hence also on the graded ring associated to \(k[[\omega_{G/k}]]\). It follows that \(ab\) is an isomorphism (C.A. V, § 7 lemma 1). Moreover, b induces an isomorphism from \(I/\overline{I^2}\) onto \(I_A/\overline{I_A^2}\), hence a surjection of the graded rings associated to \(k[[\omega_{G/k}]]\) and A (as G is radicial, \(I_A\) is contained in the radical of A, so that the intersection of the \(\overline{I_A^r}\) is zero). As \(ab\) is an isomorphism and b a surjection, b and a are isomorphisms.

Let us finally note that it is clear that (i) or (ii) imply (iv), so that it remains to prove the implication \((\mathrm{iv})\Rightarrow(\mathrm{ii})\): for this, we may assume k local, with residue field \(k_0\). Set then \(G_0 = G\widehat{\otimes}_k k_0\), \(\omega = \omega_{G/k}\), \(\omega_0 = \omega\widehat{\otimes}_k k_0\). As \(\omega_0\) is topologically free over k, there exist a topologically free k-module \(\omega'\) and a continuous k-linear map \(f : \omega' \to \omega\) such that \(f_0 = f\widehat{\otimes}_k k_0\) is invertible (f is the projective envelope of \(\omega\)). As \(\omega'\) is a projective pseudocompact k-module, f is composed of a continuous k-linear map \(g : \omega' \to I_A\) and of the canonical projection of \(I_A\) onto \(\omega\). In turn, g induces a homomorphism of topological algebras \(g : k[[\omega]] \to A\) (1.2.5). As \(\omega_0\) is identified with \(\omega_{G_0/k_0}\) and \(k_0[[\omega_0]]\) with \(k[[\omega]]\widehat{\otimes}_k k_0\), \(g_0 = g\widehat{\otimes}_k k_0\) is invertible because (iii) implies (ii) and the hypotheses (iii) are satisfied for \(k_0\) and \(G_0\). As, on the other hand, \(k[[\omega]]\) and A are projective pseudocompact k-modules, g is invertible by 0.3.4.

blocks.title: Corollaire
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.3.1

Let S be a locally noetherian scheme over \(\mathbb{Q}\) and G an S-group scheme locally of finite type. If \(\omega_{G/S}\) is a locally free \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-Module, then G is smooth over S along the unit section.

Indeed, let x be a point of the unit section, s its image in S, \(\mathscr{O}_x\) and \(\mathscr{O}_s\) the local algebras of x and s. It suffices to show that \(\widehat{\mathscr{O}}_x\) is formally smooth over \(\widehat{\mathscr{O}}_s\) (EGA IV 6.8.6 and \(0_{\mathrm{IV}}\) 19.3.6). Now \(\widehat{\mathscr{O}}_x\) and \(\widehat{\mathscr{O}}_s\) are the local rings of x and s in \(\widehat{G/S}\) and \(\widehat{S}\). Setting \(k = \widehat{\mathscr{O}}_s\) and \(H = \mathrm{Spf}\,\widehat{\mathscr{O}}_x\), it is clear that H is an infinitesimal formal group over k and that we have \(\omega_{H/k} = (\omega_{G/S})_s \otimes_{\mathscr{O}_s} \widehat{\mathscr{O}}_s\). Therefore \(\omega_{H/k}\) is the product of a finite number of copies of k. The corollary therefore follows from 3.3 and from EGA \(0_{\mathrm{IV}}\) 19.3.4.
We thus recover a result obtained elsewhere for group schemes locally of finite presentation over a scheme S.

blocks.title: Corollaire
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.3.2

If k is a field of characteristic 0, the functor \(\mathbf{L}\mapsto\mathscr{G}(\mathbf{L})\) is an equivalence from the category of k-Lie algebras to that of infinitesimal formal k-groups.

Indeed, when G runs over the infinitesimal formal k-groups, the functor \(G \mapsto \mathscr{G}(\mathrm{Lie}\,G)\) is isomorphic to the identity functor of the category of infinitesimal groups (theorem 3.3). Likewise, the functor \(\mathbf{L}\mapsto \mathrm{Lie}(\mathscr{G}(\mathbf{L}))\) is isomorphic to the identity functor of the category of Lie algebras (3.1).
Suppose still that k is a field of characteristic 0. Let \(\overline{k}\) be the algebraic closure of k and \(\Gamma\) the topological Galois group of \(\overline{k}\) over k.

For every formal k-group H, we denote by \(\underline{\mathrm{Aut}}_k H\) the group-valued k-functor which associates to every commutative k-algebra of finite dimension C the group of automorphisms of the formal C-group \(H\widehat{\otimes}_k C\). This k-functor is manifestly left exact (H is topologically flat over k!), so that we may treat it as a formal group over k. If L is a Lie algebra over k and H the formal group \(\mathscr{G}(\mathbf{L})\), theorem 3.3 shows that \(\underline{\mathrm{Aut}}_k H\) is isomorphic to the group-valued k-functor \(\underline{\mathrm{Aut}}_k \mathbf{L}\) which associates to a k-algebra of finite dimension C the group of automorphisms of the C-Lie algebra \(C\otimes_k \mathbf{L}\).

If G is an arbitrary formal k-group, we saw in 2.5.2 that G decomposes canonically into a semidirect product of an étale formal group \(G_e\) and an infinitesimal formal group \(G_0\). This semidirect product is determined by the data of Lie G, of the \(\Gamma\)-group \(G_e(\overline{k})\) and of a homomorphism \(h : G_e \to \underline{\mathrm{Aut}}_k(\mathrm{Lie}\,G)\). Such a homomorphism sends \(G_e\) into the "étale part" \((\underline{\mathrm{Aut}}_k\,\mathrm{Lie}\,G)_e\) of \(\underline{\mathrm{Aut}}_k\,\mathrm{Lie}\,G\) (2.5.1). It is therefore determined by the data of a homomorphism of \(\Gamma\)-groups \[ h(\overline{k}) : G(\overline{k}) \longrightarrow (\underline{\mathrm{Aut}}_k \mathbf{L})(\overline{k}). \]

If one makes \(\Gamma\) act on \((\mathrm{Lie}\,G)\otimes_k\overline{k}\) by means of the formula \(\gamma(\ell\otimes x) = \ell\otimes\gamma(x)\), the homomorphism \(h(\overline{k})\) makes \(G(\overline{k})\) act by automorphisms on the k-Lie algebra \((\mathrm{Lie}\,G)\otimes_k\overline{k}\). We express this last condition by saying that \(G(\overline{k})\) acts on \((\mathrm{Lie}\,G)\otimes_k\overline{k}\) in a manner compatible with \(\Gamma\).

One can summarize the situation with the help of a "learned" statement: call a \(\Gamma\)-Lie algebra over k the data of a triple \((\mathbf{L},K,h)\) formed of a Lie algebra \(\mathbf{L}\) over k, a \(\Gamma\)-group K and an action h of K on \(\mathbf{L}\otimes_k\overline{k}\) which is "compatible with the action of \(\Gamma\) on K and \(\mathbf{L}\otimes_k\overline{k}\)". If \((\mathbf{L},K,h)\) and \((\mathbf{L}',K',h')\) are such \(\Gamma\)-Lie algebras, a homomorphism from the first to the second is a pair (f,g) formed of a morphism \(f : \mathbf{L}\to \mathbf{L}'\) of Lie algebras and a morphism \(g : K\to K'\) of \(\Gamma\)-groups such that \((f\otimes_k\overline{k})(\gamma\cdot x) = g(\gamma)\cdot(f\otimes_k\overline{k})(x)\) if \(x\in \mathbf{L}\otimes_k\overline{k}\) and \(\gamma\in K\): then the functor \(G\mapsto (\mathrm{Lie}\,G, G(\overline{k}), h(\overline{k}))\) is an equivalence from the category of formal groups over k (= field of characteristic 0) to the category of \(\Gamma\)-Lie algebras.
4. Phenomena particular to characteristic p > 0
Throughout this section 4, we denote by p a prime number, by k a pseudocompact ring of characteristic p, and by \(\pi\) the continuous endomorphism \(x\mapsto x^p\) of k.

Let X be a formal variety over k with affine algebra A and \(X^{(p/k)}\) or \(X^{(p)}\) the formal variety \(X\widehat{\otimes}_\pi k\) (cf. 1.2). This variety has for affine algebra the completed tensor product \(A\widehat{\otimes}_\pi k\); moreover, the continuous homomorphism from \(A\widehat{\otimes}_\pi k\) to A which maps \(a\widehat{\otimes}_\pi\lambda\) to \(a^p\lambda\) induces a morphism of formal k-varieties \[ \mathrm{Fr}(X/k) : X \longrightarrow X^{(p/k)}. \] In what follows, we shall say that Fr(X/k) is the Frobenius morphism of X relative to k and we shall often write Fr instead of Fr(X/k).

Consider a scheme S over the prime field \(\mathbb{F}_p\), an S-scheme X and an S-scheme \(q : T\to S\) of finite length (1.2.6). Let fr(S) be the Frobenius morphism of S relative to \(\mathbb{F}_p\) (this morphism induces the identity on the underlying topological space and maps a section s of the structure sheaf to \(s^p\); cf. VIIA 4.1). If we set \(X^{(p)} = X\times_{\mathrm{fr}(S)} S\) (VIIA, loc. cit.), the morphisms of formal S-varieties \(f : T\to \widehat{X^{(p)}}/\widehat{S}\) correspond bijectively to the S-morphisms from T to \(X^{(p)}\) (1.2.6), that is to say to the commutative squares \[ \xymatrix{ T \ar[r]^-{f'} \ar[d]_-q & X \ar[d] \\ S \ar[r]_-{\mathrm{fr}(S)} & S } \] .

To such a square is associated an S-morphism from T to \((\widehat{X}/\widehat{S})^{(p)}\) if and only if \(f'\) factors through a subscheme \(X_0\) of X which is of finite length over S. One thus sees that \(\mathrm{Hom}_{\mathbf{Vf}/\widehat{S}}(T,(\widehat{X}/\widehat{S})^{(p)})\) can be identified with a subset of \(\mathrm{Hom}_{\mathbf{Vf}/\widehat{S}}(T,\widehat{X^{(p)}}/\widehat{S})\), which defines \((\widehat{X}/\widehat{S})^{(p)}\) as a formal subvariety of \(\widehat{X^{(p)}}/\widehat{S}\). We have the equality \((\widehat{X}/\widehat{S})^{(p)} = \widehat{X^{(p)}}/\widehat{S}\) if \(\mathrm{fr}(S)\circ q\) is an S-scheme of finite length whenever q is one. This occurs, for example, when S is locally of finite type over a field k such that \([k:k^p] < +\infty\).

Let G be a formal group over k with affine algebra A. As the functor \(X\mapsto X\widehat{\otimes}_\pi k\) commutes with finite products, it transforms a formal k-group into a formal k-group. Furthermore, as Fr(X/k) is functorial in X, the morphism \[ \mathrm{Fr} : G \longrightarrow G^{(p)} \] is a homomorphism of formal k-groups. If we set \(G^{(p^{n+1})} = (G^{(p^n)})^{(p)}\), the same holds for the composite morphism \[ \mathrm{Fr}^n = \mathrm{Fr}^n(G/k) : G \xrightarrow{\ \mathrm{F}\ } G^{(p)} \xrightarrow{\ \mathrm{F}\ } G^{(p^2)} \xrightarrow{\ \mathrm{F}\ } \cdots \xrightarrow{\ \mathrm{F}\ } G^{(p^n)}. \] The kernel of this homomorphism \(\mathrm{Fr}^n\) will be denoted \({}_{\mathrm{Fr}^n}G\). It follows directly from the definitions that \({}_{\mathrm{Fr}^n}G\) is radicial and has for affine algebra the quotient \(A/I_A^{\{p^n\}}\), where \(I_A^{\{p^n\}}\) denotes the closed ideal of A generated by the \(p^n\)-th powers of the elements of \(I_A\). When \(I_A^{\{p^n\}}\) is zero, that is to say when we have \({}_{\mathrm{Fr}^n}G = G\), we say that G is of height \(\leqslant n\).

It is clear that the Lie algebra \(\mathrm{Lie}(G)\) of a formal k-group G is a p-Lie subalgebra of the Algebra \(\mathbf{H}(G)\) (cf. 2.3): indeed, one reduces at once to the case where k is artinian; in this case, Lie(G) is the subset of H(G) formed of the elements x such that \(\varphi_G(x\otimes1+1\otimes x) = \Delta_G(x)\) with the notation of 2.3 and 2.6 (c). We then have \[ \begin{aligned} \varphi_G(x^p\otimes1+1\otimes x^p) &= \varphi_G\big((x\otimes1+1\otimes x)^p\big)\\ &= \varphi_G(x\otimes1+1\otimes x)^p = \Delta_G(x)^p = \Delta_G(x^p). \end{aligned} \]

Conversely, every p-Lie algebra L over \(\mathbf{O}_k\) defines a group-valued k-functor: indeed, denote by \(\mathbf{U}_p(\mathbf{L})\) the functor which associates to every k-algebra of finite length C the restricted enveloping algebra \(\mathbf{U}_p(\mathbf{L}(C))\) of the p-Lie algebra \(\mathbf{L}(C)\) over C (VIIA 5.3). By VIIA 5.4, \(\mathbf{U}_p(\mathbf{L})\) is an \(\mathbf{O}_k\)-bialgebra and determines a group-valued k-functor \(\mathrm{Spf}^*\mathbf{U}_p(\mathbf{L})\) (2.2) which we shall from now on denote \(\mathscr{G}_p(\mathbf{L})\): \(\mathscr{G}_p(\mathbf{L})(C)\) is thus the group of elements \(z\in \mathbf{U}_p(\mathbf{L}(C))\) of augmentation 1 and such that \(\Delta_{\mathbf{U}_p(\mathbf{L}(C))}(z) = z\otimes z\).

When \(\mathbf{L}\) is a flat p-Lie algebra over \(\mathbf{O}_k\), \(\mathbf{U}_p(\mathbf{L})\) is flat over k by VIIA 5.3.3; consequently, \(\mathscr{G}_p(\mathbf{L})\) is a formal group topologically flat over k which has \(\mathbf{U}_p(\mathbf{L})\) as its \(\mathbf{O}_k\)-bialgebra. This formal group can be characterized by a universal property: indeed, consider a homomorphism h from \(\mathscr{G}_p(\mathbf{L})\) into a formal group G; such an h induces a homomorphism \(\mathbf{Lie}\,h\) from the Lie Algebra of \(\mathscr{G}_p(\mathbf{L})\) into that of G. One sees on the other hand, as in VIIA 7.3, that the canonical morphism from \(\mathbf{L}\) into the restricted enveloping Algebra \(\mathbf{U}_p(\mathbf{L})\) defines an isomorphism from \(\mathbf{L}\) onto the p-Lie algebra of \(\mathscr{G}_p(\mathbf{L})\); composing this isomorphism with \(\mathbf{Lie}\,h\), one obtains a homomorphism \(h' : \mathbf{L}\to \mathrm{Lie}\,G\):

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition

If k is a pseudocompact ring of characteristic \(p>0\), G a formal k-group and \(\mathbf{L}\) a flat p-Lie algebra over \(\mathbf{O}_k\), the map \(h\mapsto h'\) defined above is a bijection \[ \mathrm{Hom}_{k\text{-Gr.}}(\mathscr{G}_p(\mathbf{L}), G) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Hom}_p(\mathbf{L}, \mathrm{Lie}\,G) \] from the set of homomorphisms of formal k-groups from \(\mathscr{G}_p(\mathbf{L})\) to G onto that of morphisms of p-Lie algebras from \(\mathbf{L}\) to Lie G.

Indeed, one reduces at once to the case where k is artinian. In this case, the set \(\mathrm{Hom}_{k\text{-Gr.}}(\mathscr{G}_p(\mathbf{L}),G)\) is in canonical one-to-one correspondence (2.3.1) with the homomorphisms of unitary algebras \(g : \mathbf{U}_p(\mathbf{L})\to \mathbf{H}(G)\) such that the following diagram is commutative: \[ \xymatrix{ \mathbf{U}_p(\mathbf{L}) \ar[rr]^-{g} \ar[d]_-{\Delta} & & \mathbf{H}(G) \ar[dr]^-{\delta_G} & \\ \mathbf{U}_p(\mathbf{L})\otimes\mathbf{U}_p(\mathbf{L}) \ar[rr]_-{g\otimes g} & & \mathbf{H}(G)\otimes\mathbf{H}(G) \ar[r]_-{\varphi_G} & \mathbf{H}(G\times G) } \] Now g is defined by its restriction to \(\mathbf{L}\), which is a homomorphism of p-Lie algebras from \(\mathbf{L}\) into the p-Lie algebra underlying \(\mathbf{H}(G)\). The commutativity of the diagram therefore means simply that g maps \(\mathbf{L}\) into the subset Lie G of \(\mathbf{H}(G)\) formed of the x such that \(\varphi_G(x\otimes1+1\otimes x) = \delta_G x\).
We now propose to study in more detail the formal k-group \(\mathscr{G}_p(\mathbf{L})\) when \(\mathbf{L}\) is a flat p-Lie algebra over \(\mathbf{O}_k\).

For this, we first consider a ring C of characteristic p and a p-Lie algebra \(\mathbf{L}\) over C whose underlying module is free with basis \((x_i)_{i\in I}\). Denote moreover by P the set of families \(n=(n_i)_{i\in I}\) formed of natural integers such that \(0\leqslant n_i < p\) and such that the \(n_i\) are zero except perhaps for a finite number of them. If we equip I with a total order and if we call \(i_1,i_2,\ldots,i_r\) (\(i_1<i_2<\cdots<i_r\)) the indices i such that \(n_i\neq0\), we may thus set \(|n|=n_{i_1}+\cdots+n_{i_r}\) and \[ x^n = x_{i_1}^{n_{i_1}}\cdot x_{i_2}^{n_{i_2}}\cdots x_{i_r}^{n_{i_r}}, \qquad n! = (n_{i_1}!)\cdots(n_{i_r}!). \] It is known that the monomials \(x^n/n!\) form a basis of \(\mathbf{U}_p(\mathbf{L})\) (VIIA 5.3.3) and it is clear that we have \[ (*)\qquad \Delta\left(\frac{x^n}{n!}\right) = \sum_{m\leqslant n} \frac{x^m}{m!}\otimes\frac{x^{n-m}}{(n-m)!} \] the sum being extended over all \(m\in\mathrm{P}\) such that \(m\leqslant n\) (i.e. \(m_i\leqslant n_i\) for every \(i\in I\)).

Formula (*) allows an easy determination of the natural filtration of \(\mathbf{U}_p(\mathbf{L})\) (cf. 1.3.6). Let \(\mathrm{U}^+\) be the two-sided ideal of \(\mathbf{U}_p(\mathbf{L})\) generated by \(\mathbf{L}\) and set \(\mathrm{U}=\mathbf{U}_p(\mathbf{L})\). Recall that by definition we have \[ \mathrm{U}_0 = C\cdot 1_{\mathrm{U}} \] \[ \ldots\ldots\ldots\ldots \] \[ \mathrm{U}_n = \{u\in \mathrm{U}\mid \Delta_{\mathrm{U}}u - u\otimes1 \in \mathrm{U}_{n-1}\otimes \mathrm{U}^+\}. \] Formula (*) then implies that \(\mathrm{U}_n\) is the free C-module having for basis the monomials \(x^m\) such that \(|m|\leqslant n\).

With the notation of 4.3, let us determine the elements \(\xi\) of U of augmentation 1 and such that \(\Delta_{\mathrm{U}}\xi = \xi\otimes\xi\): every element \(\xi\) of U can be put in the form \(\xi = \sum_{n\in\mathrm{P}} \xi_n\frac{x^n}{n!}\), where \(\xi_n\in C\). The condition \(\varepsilon_{\mathrm{U}}(\xi)=1\) implies the equality \(\xi_0=1\), if 0 denotes the element of P all of whose components are zero. The condition \(\Delta_{\mathbf{U}_p(\mathbf{L})}(\xi) = \xi\otimes\xi\) implies \[ \sum_{m\leqslant n} \xi_n\frac{x^m}{m!}\otimes\frac{x^{n-m}}{(n-m)!} = \sum_{q,r} \xi_q\xi_r \frac{x^q}{q!}\otimes\frac{x^r}{r!} \] that is to say \[ \xi_{q+r} = \xi_q\xi_r \ \text{ if } q_i+r_i<p \qquad\text{and}\qquad \xi_q\xi_r = 0 \ \text{ otherwise.} \] If we denote by \(\xi_i\) the value of \(\xi_n\) when \(n_i=1\) and \(n_j=0\) for \(j\neq i\), these conditions mean that we have \[ \xi_n = \prod_i \xi_i^{n_i} \ \text{ if } n\in\mathrm{P} \qquad\text{and}\qquad \xi_i^p = 0\ \ \forall i\in I. \]
From this one obtains:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition

Let p be a prime number, k a pseudocompact ring of characteristic p, \(\mathbf{L}\) a flat p-Lie algebra over \(\mathbf{O}_k\), C a profinite k-algebra of finite length and \(\sqrt[p]{0_C}\) the ideal of C formed of the elements x such that \(x^p=0\). There exists a bijection \[ \mathbf{L}(C)\otimes_C \sqrt[p]{0_C} \longrightarrow \mathscr{G}_p(\mathbf{L})(C) \] "which is functorial in C".

Indeed, by 1.2.3, one can choose a basis \({}^C(x_i)_{i\in I}\) of \(\mathbf{L}(C)\) over C in such a way that, for every morphism \(\varphi : C\to D\) of \(\mathrm{Alf}/k\) (0.4.2), \(\mathbf{L}(\varphi)\) maps \({}^Cx_i\) to \({}^Dx_i\). By what precedes, the map \(\sum_i {}^Cx_i\otimes\xi_i \mapsto \sum_{n\in\mathrm{P}} \Big(\prod_i \xi_i^{n_i}\Big)\frac{{}^Cx^n}{n!}\) is a functorial bijection from \(\mathbf{L}(C)\otimes_C \sqrt[p]{0_C}\) onto \(\mathscr{G}_p(\mathbf{L})(C)\).

"There is no Campbell-Hausdorff formula in characteristic \(p>0\)"; let us explain: the functorial isomorphism of 4.3.1 depends on the choice of the bases \({}^C(x_i)_{i\in I}\). Unlike what happens in 3.2 (the case of characteristic 0), there is in general no bijection from \(\mathbf{L}(C)\otimes_C\sqrt[p]{0_C}\) onto \(\mathscr{G}_p(\mathbf{L})(C)\) which is "functorial both in C and in \(\mathbf{L}\)". The following argument shows, for example, that such an isomorphism does not exist when k is a field containing the \((p^2-1)\)-th roots of unity:

Indeed, choose \(\mathbf{L}\) in such a way that, for every \(C\in\mathrm{Alf}/k\), \(\mathbf{L}(C)\) is the abelian p-Lie algebra over C generated by an element x subject to the relation \(x^{(p^2)}=0\). We thus have \[ \mathbf{L}(C) = Cx \oplus Cx^{(p)}. \] We shall show that the only functorial morphism \[ \chi_C : \mathbf{L}(C)\otimes_C \sqrt[p]{0_C} \longrightarrow \mathbf{U}_p(\mathbf{L}(C)), \] which is compatible with the endomorphisms of \(\mathbf{L}\) and which maps \(\mathbf{L}(C)\otimes_C\sqrt[p]{0_C}\) into \(\mathscr{G}_p(\mathbf{L})(C)\), is the constant morphism with value 1. Indeed, let \(\psi_C\) be the map from \(\mathbf{L}(C)\otimes_C\sqrt[p]{0_C}\) into \(\mathbf{U}_p(\mathbf{L}(C))\) which sends \(x\otimes a+x^{(p)}\otimes b\) to \(\sum_{0\leqslant i,j<p} a^ib^j x^{i+pj}\). Composing \(\chi_C\) with \(\psi_C^{-1}\), one obtains a morphism functorial (in C): \[ \mathbf{L}(C)\otimes_C\sqrt[p]{0_C} \longrightarrow \mathbf{L}(C)\otimes_C\sqrt[p]{0_C} \] which maps \(x\otimes a+x^{(p)}\otimes b\) to \(x\otimes \mathrm{P}(a,b)+x^{(p)}\otimes \mathrm{Q}(a,b)\). Functoriality in C implies that \(\mathrm{P}(a,b)\) and \(\mathrm{Q}(a,b)\) are the values at \((a,b)\) of two polynomials \(\mathrm{P},\mathrm{Q}\in k[X,Y]\) which we may assume of degree \(<p\) in X as well as in Y. If \(\alpha\) is an element of k and if \(\underline{\ell}\) is the endomorphism of \(\mathbf{L}\) which maps x to \(\alpha x\), one sees at once that the square \[ \xymatrix{ \mathbf{L}(C)\otimes_C\sqrt[p]{0_C} \ar[r]^-{\psi_C} \ar[d]_-{\underline{\ell}(C)\otimes_C\sqrt[p]{0_C}} & \mathbf{U}_p(\mathbf{L}(C)) \ar[d]^-{\mathbf{U}_p(\underline{\ell}(C))} \\ \mathbf{L}(C)\otimes_C\sqrt[p]{0_C} \ar[r]_-{\psi_C} & \mathbf{U}_p(\mathbf{L}(C)) } \] is commutative. Taking into account the compatibility of \(\chi_C\) with \(\underline{\ell}\), one deduces the equalities \(\mathrm{P}(\alpha a,\alpha^pb)=\alpha\mathrm{P}(a,b)\) and \(\mathrm{Q}(\alpha a,\alpha^pb)=\alpha^p\mathrm{Q}(a,b)\). Taking for \(\alpha\) a primitive \((p^2-1)\)-th root of unity and taking for C the algebra \(k[X,Y]/(X^p,Y^p)\), one

deduces that P and Q are of the form \(\mathrm{P}=\lambda X\) and \(\mathrm{Q}=\mu Y\), \(\lambda,\mu\in k\). Consequently \(\chi_C\) sends \(x\otimes a+x^{(p)}\otimes b\) to \(\sum_{0\leqslant i,j<p} (\lambda a)^i(\mu b)^j x^{i+pj}\).
This last map "commutes" with the endomorphism of \(\mathbf{L}\) which sends x to \(x^{(p)}\) only if \(\lambda=\mu=0\).

blocks.title: Théorème
blocks.html:
Theorem 4.4

Let p be a prime number, k a pseudocompact ring of characteristic p and G a formal group over k. The following assertions are equivalent:

  1. (i)

    There exists a p-Lie algebra \(\mathbf{L}\) flat over \(\mathbf{O}_k\) such that G is isomorphic to \(\mathscr{G}_p(\mathbf{L})\).

  2. (ii)

    There exists a projective pseudocompact k-module \(\omega\) such that the affine algebra of G is isomorphic to the quotient of \(k[[\omega]]\) by the closed ideal generated by the \(x^p\), \(x\in\omega\).

  3. (iii)

    G is of height \(\leqslant1\) and \(\omega_{G/k}\) is a projective pseudocompact k-module.

  4. (iv)

    G is of height \(\leqslant1\) and is topologically flat over k.

(i)\(\Rightarrow\)(ii): Let \(\omega\) be the pseudocompact k-module dual to \(\mathbf{L}\) (1.2.3) and A the quotient of \(k[[\omega]]\) by the closed ideal generated by the \(x^p\), \(x\in\omega\). For every profinite k-algebra of finite length C, a continuous homomorphism \(h : A\to C\) is determined by its restriction \(h'\) to \(\omega\); this restriction \(h'\) sends \(\omega\) into \(\sqrt[p]{0_C}\); one thus obtains a canonical bijection from \(\mathrm{Hom}_{\mathrm{Al}/k}(A,C)\) onto the set \(\mathrm{Hom}_k(\omega,\sqrt[p]{0_C})\) of continuous k-linear maps from \(\omega\) into \(\sqrt[p]{0_C}\). This last set is canonically isomorphic to \(\mathbf{L}(C)\otimes_C \sqrt[p]{0_C}\) (see the proof of 3.3). The implication (i)\(\Rightarrow\)(ii) therefore follows from 4.3.1.
For the other implications, consult the proofs of theorems 3.3 and VIIA 7.4.1, which are analogous.

blocks.title: Corollaire
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.4.1

If k is a field of characteristic \(p>0\), the functor \(\mathbf{L}\mapsto \mathscr{G}_p(\mathbf{L})\) is an equivalence from the category of p-Lie algebras over k to that of formal k-groups of height \(\leqslant1\).

Indeed, when G runs over the formal groups of height \(\leqslant1\), the functor \(G\mapsto \mathscr{G}_p(\mathrm{Lie}\,G)\) is isomorphic to the identity functor by theorem 4.4; likewise, we have seen that the functor \(\mathbf{L}\mapsto \mathrm{Lie}\,\mathscr{G}_p(\mathbf{L})\) is isomorphic to the identity functor (confer VIIA 7.5).

Let us again take for k a field of characteristic p. For every infinitesimal formal k-group G, the affine algebra A of G is the projective limit of the affine algebras \(A/I_A^{\{p^n\}}\) of the groups \({}_{\mathrm{Fr}^n}G\) (cf. 4.1.2). An infinitesimal formal k-group is therefore an inductive limit of formal k-groups of finite height.

Suppose now that G is of height \(\leqslant n\). As \({}_{\mathrm{Fr}}G\) is the kernel of \(\mathrm{Fr} : G\to G^{(p)}\), Fr induces a monomorphism i from \(H=G/{}_{\mathrm{Fr}}G\) into \(G^{(p)}\). As \(\mathrm{Fr}^n(G/k) = \mathrm{Fr}^{n-1}(G^{(p)}/k)\circ\mathrm{Fr}(G/k)\) is zero, \(\mathrm{Fr}^{n-1}(G^{(p)}/k)\circ i\) is zero, as is \(i^{(p^{n-1})}\circ\mathrm{Fr}^{n-1}(H/k)\). As i is a monomorphism (2.4.1), one sees that H is of height \(\leqslant n-1\). One thus sees that a group of finite height possesses a composition series whose quotients are of height \(\leqslant1\), hence can be described by p-Lie algebras over k.

Finally, when G is an infinitesimal formal k-group such that \(\omega_{G/k}\) is of finite dimension over k, the affine algebra A of G is a quotient of \(k[[\omega]]\), so that all the algebras \(A/I_A^{\{p^n\}}\) are of finite dimension over k as vector spaces. One consequently sees that an infinitesimal formal group over a field of characteristic \(p>0\) is an inductive limit of finite formal groups (i.e. of finite length, 1.2.6) as soon as \(\omega_{G/k}\) is of finite dimension over k.

pages.title: 5. Homogeneous spaces of infinitesimal formal groups over a field

blocks.html: 5. Homogeneous spaces of infinitesimal formal groups over a field
In all that follows, the letter k denotes a field.

Let G be an infinitesimal formal k-group with affine algebra A, B a closed subalgebra of A, \(I_B\) the intersection \(B\cap I_A\) of B with the augmentation ideal of A, \(\overline{AI_B}\) the closed ideal of A generated by \(I_B\), X the formal spectrum of B and \(r : G\to X\) the epimorphism induced by the inclusion of B into A. We propose to see under what condition r makes X the left quotient of G by a subgroup F (2.4):

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition

With the notation above, the two following assertions are equivalent:

  1. (i)

    \(\forall x\in B,\ \Delta_A x - 1\widehat{\otimes}x \in \overline{AI_B}\widehat{\otimes}_k A\).

  2. (ii)

    The diagonal morphism of A induces, by passage to the quotient, a diagonal morphism on the algebra \(A/AI_B\) which makes the formal spectrum of this algebra a formal subgroup of G. Moreover, if q denotes the canonical projection of A onto \(A/\overline{AI_B}\), the sequence \[ (*)\qquad B \xrightarrow{\ \mathrm{incl.}\ } A \xrightarrow{\ \mathrm{in}_2 - (q\widehat{\otimes}A)\Delta_A\ } (A/\overline{AI_B})\widehat{\otimes}_k A \] is exact; in other words, r induces an isomorphism from \(F\backslash G\) onto X.

Assertion (i) means simply that B is contained in the kernel of the pair \((\mathrm{in}_2,(q\widehat{\otimes}A)\Delta_A)\). Consequently (ii) implies (i). The proof of the converse occupies paragraphs 5.1.1 to 5.1.5.

Consider first the following category (Cou): an object of (Cou) is a pair (C,J) formed of a profinite k-algebra C and a closed ideal J of C; a morphism \(\psi : (C,I)\to(D,J)\) of (Cou) is a continuous homomorphism from the algebra C to the algebra D which maps I into J. If one associates to (C,I) the pair \((\mathrm{Spf}(C/I),\mathrm{Spf}\,C)\), one obviously obtains an antiequivalence from (Cou) to the category of pairs (Z,Y) formed of a formal k-variety Y and a formal subvariety Z.
A co-group structure on an object (C,J) of (Cou) consists of the data of a formal group structure on Spf C such that the following conditions are satisfied (notation of 2.1):

1. (1)

    \(\Delta_C J \subset J\widehat{\otimes}_kC + C\widehat{\otimes}_kJ\) ;

  2. (2)

    \(\varepsilon_C(J) = 0\) ;

  3. (3)

    \(c_C(J) \subset J\).
These conditions also mean that Spf(C/J) is a formal subgroup of Spf C.

It follows from 2.7 that conditions 2) and 3) are a consequence of 1) when C is local, that is to say when Spf C is an infinitesimal group. In particular, if we set \(I = \overline{AI_B}\), condition (i) of proposition 5.1 implies the relation \[ \Delta_A I_B \subset I\widehat{\otimes}_kA + A\widehat{\otimes}_kI \] whence \[ \Delta_A I \subset I\widehat{\otimes}_kA + A\widehat{\otimes}_kI, \] so that \(\Delta_A\) induces a diagonal morphism in A/I which makes A/I the affine algebra of a formal subgroup F of G.

Denote by \(\mathrm{GrAl}/k\) the category of "graded profinite" k-algebras: an object of this category consists of the data of a sequence \(A_0,A_1,\ldots,A_n,\ldots\) of linearly compact vector spaces over k and of a structure of profinite algebra on the product \(\prod_{n\geqslant0}A_n\) such that \(A_n\cdot A_m \subset A_{m+n}\) (\(A_n\) being identified with a subset of \(\prod_{i\geqslant0}A_i\) by means of the canonical injection); a morphism \(\psi : (A_n)\to(B_n)\) is a sequence of continuous linear maps \(\psi_n : A_n\to B_n\) such that \(\psi_{m+n}(a\cdot a') = \psi_m(a)\cdot\psi_n(a')\) if \(a\in A_m\) and \(a'\in A_n\).

It is clear that two graded profinite k-algebras \((A_n)\) and \((B_n)\) have a direct sum in \(\mathrm{GrAl}/k\) and that this direct sum has for n-th component the product \(\prod_{i=0}^n A_i\widehat{\otimes}_kB_{n-i}\) of the linearly compact k-vector spaces \(A_i\widehat{\otimes}_kB_{n-i}\). This direct sum will be denoted \((A_n)\widehat{\otimes}_k(B_n)\).

To every object (C,J) of (Cou) one can associate the graded profinite algebra \(\mathrm{Gr}_J(C)\) of C for the filtration defined by the closures \(\overline{J^n}\) of the powers of J; we thus have \(\mathrm{Gr}_J(C)_n = J^n/J^{n+1}\) and the multiplication of \(\mathrm{Gr}_J(C)\) is induced by that of C. Moreover, it follows from the lemma below that the functor \((C,J)\mapsto \mathrm{Gr}_J(C)\) commutes with finite direct sums; it therefore transforms a co-group of (Cou) into a co-group of \(\mathrm{GrAl}/k\). One sees in particular that, with the notation of 5.1.1, the graded profinite algebra \(\mathrm{Gr}_I(A)\) is equipped with a co-group structure in \(\mathrm{GrAl}/k\):

blocks.title: Lemme
blocks.html:
Lemma

Let \(U_0\supset U_1\supset\cdots\) and \(V_0\supset V_1\supset\cdots\) be two separated filtered linearly compact k-vector spaces. Filter the completed tensor product \(W = U\widehat{\otimes}_kV\) by means of the closed subspaces \[ W_n = U_n\widehat{\otimes}_kV_0 + U_{n-1}\widehat{\otimes}_kV_1 + \cdots + U_0\widehat{\otimes}_kV_n. \] For every n, the canonical maps from \((U_i/U_{i+1})\widehat{\otimes}_k(V_j/V_{j+1})\) into \(W_n/W_{n+1}\) (\(i+j=n\)) define an isomorphism \[ \bigoplus_{i+j=n} (U_i/U_{i+1})\widehat{\otimes}_k(V_j/V_{j+1}) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } W_n/W_{n+1}. \]

Identify every profinite k-algebra \(\Gamma\) with the graded profinite k-algebra \((\Gamma_n)_{n\geqslant0}\) such that \(\Gamma_0=\Gamma\) and \(\Gamma_n=0\) if \(n>0\). In particular, if \((A_n)_{n>0}\) is a graded profinite k-algebra, we shall regard \(A_0\) indifferently as a profinite k-algebra or as a graded profinite k-algebra. We shall then denote by \(\rho : (A_n)\to A_0\) the morphism of \(\mathrm{GrAl}/k\) such that \(\rho_0=\mathrm{id}_{A_0}\) and \(\rho_n=0\) if \(n>0\). Likewise, \(\iota : A_0\to(A_n)\) will denote the section of \(\rho\) such that \(\iota_0=\mathrm{id}_{A_0}\) and \(\iota_n=0\) if \(n>0\).

Every cogroup structure on \((A_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}}\) induces a cogroup structure on \(A_0\) such that \(\rho\) and \(\iota\) are homomorphisms of cogroups. As \(\iota\) is a section of \(\rho\), the cogroup \((A_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}}\) is therefore a "semidirect sum" of \(A_0\) and a cogroup \((A'_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}}\) of \(\mathrm{GrAl}/k\) such that \(A'_0=k\). In other words, if \((A'_n)\) is the graded subalgebra of \((A_n)\) such that the sequence \[ (A'_n) \xrightarrow{\ j=\mathrm{incl.}\ } (A_n) \xrightarrow{\ \mathrm{in}_2 - (\rho\widehat{\otimes}\mathrm{Id})\Delta\ } A_0\widehat{\otimes}_k(A_n) \] is exact, the morphism \((A_0\widehat{\otimes}_kA'_n)\to(A_n)\) with components \(\iota\) and j is invertible. (We denote by \(\Delta : (A_n)\to(A_n)\widehat{\otimes}_k(A_n)\) the morphism defining the cogroup structure of \((A_n)\)).

In the case which interests us, we have \((A_n) = \mathrm{Gr}_I(A)\) and \(A_0 = A/I\). Moreover, it is clear that the algebra \((A_n)\) is generated by \(A_0\) and \(A_1\), that is to say that, for \(n\geqslant1\), the map \[ A_1\widehat{\otimes}_{A_0}A_1\widehat{\otimes}_{A_0}\cdots\widehat{\otimes}_{A_0}A_1 \longrightarrow A_n \] defined by the multiplication is surjective. As we have \(A_1 \simeq A_0\widehat{\otimes}_kA'_1\) and \(A_n \simeq A_0\widehat{\otimes}_kA'_n\), hence also \(A_1\widehat{\otimes}_{A_0}A_1\widehat{\otimes}_{A_0}\cdots\widehat{\otimes}_{A_0}A_1 \simeq A_0\widehat{\otimes}_k(A'_1\widehat{\otimes}_k\cdots\widehat{\otimes}_kA'_1)\), one sees that the map \(A'_1\widehat{\otimes}_k\cdots\widehat{\otimes}_kA'_1 \to A'_n\), induced by the multiplication of \((A'_n)\), is surjective; in other words, \((A'_n)\) is generated by its terms of degree 1.

Denote now by Gr(B) and Gr'(B) the graded rings associated to B for the filtration induced by that of A and for the filtration defined by the closures \(\overline{I_B^n}\) of the powers of \(I_B\). As the diagram \[ \xymatrix{ B \ar[r]^-{\mathrm{incl.}} & A \ar[r]^-{\mathrm{in}_2 - (q\widehat{\otimes}_kA)\Delta_A} & (A/\overline{AI_B})\widehat{\otimes}_kA } \] is commutative by condition (i), one sees that we have \(\mathrm{Gr}(B)_n \subset A'_n\) for every n. Now \(I/\overline{I^2} \simeq (A/I)\widehat{\otimes}_kA'_1\) is generated, as a pseudocompact (A/I)-module, by the image of \(\mathrm{Gr}'(B)_1\), so that we have \(\mathrm{Gr}(B)_1 \simeq A'_1\), whence \(\mathrm{Gr}(B) = (A'_n)\); moreover, the canonical map from \(\mathrm{Gr}'(B)\) to \(\mathrm{Gr}(B)\) is surjective since the graded algebra \(\mathrm{Gr}(B) = (A'_n)\) is generated by its terms of degree 1. Lemma 5.1.5 therefore shows that we have \(\mathrm{Gr}'(B) = \mathrm{Gr}(B)\).

Consider finally the kernel N of the pair \((\mathrm{in}_2,(q\widehat{\otimes}A)\Delta_A)\) above and equip N with the filtration induced by that of A. We then have \((A'_n)\subset \mathrm{Gr}(B) \subset \mathrm{Gr}(N) \subset (A'_n)\). As all the filtrations considered are separated, we deduce \(N=B\) (C.A., V, §7, lemma 1).

blocks.title: Lemme
blocks.html:
Lemma 5.1.5

Let B be an abelian group, \(B=B'_0\supset B'_1\supset B'_2\supset\cdots\) and \(B=B_0\supset B_1\supset B_2\supset\cdots\) two separated filtrations of B by subgroups such that \(B'_m\subset B_m\) for every m. If the canonical map from \(B'_m/B'_{m+2}\) to \(B_m/B_{m+1}\) is surjective for every m and if B is complete for the topology defined by the filtration \((B'_m)\), then we have \(B'_m = B_m\) for every m.
Apply lemma 1 of C.A. V, §7 to the inclusion of \(B'_m\) in \(B_m\).
In all the remainder of this section 5, k denotes a perfect field of characteristic \(p>0\).

We set \(\overline{\mathbb{N}} = \mathbb{N}\cup\{\infty\}\). If B is a profinite k-algebra and if \(r\in\overline{\mathbb{N}}\), we denote by \(((x^{p^r}))_{x\in\mathfrak{r}(B)}\) the closed ideal of B generated by the elements \(x^{p^r}\), where x runs over the radical \(\mathfrak{r}(B)\) of B. If \(r=\infty\), we use the same notation with the convention that \(((x^{p^\infty}))_{x\in\mathfrak{r}(B)}\) is the zero ideal. In both cases, \(B_r\) denotes the quotient \(B/((x^{p^r}))_{x\in\mathfrak{r}(B)}\).
We say that B is of height \(\leqslant r\) if \(((x^{p^r}))_{x\in\mathfrak{r}(B)}\) is the zero ideal; if this occurs and if r is finite, we say that B is of finite height.

Consider in particular the case where B is of the form \(k[[\omega]]\), \(\omega\) being a linearly compact k-vector space (0.2.2). We then say that B is a formal power series algebra and that \(B_r\) (\(r\in\overline{\mathbb{N}}\); we thus agree to say that \(B_\infty=B\) is also "truncated") is a truncated formal power series algebra. If \(B=k[[\omega]]\), we also write \(((x^{p^r}))_{x\in\omega}\) instead of \(((x^{p^r}))_{x\in\mathfrak{r}(B)}\).

When \(\omega\) is a linearly compact k-vector space filtered by an increasing sequence of closed vector subspaces \[ 0=\omega_0\subset\omega_1\subset\omega_2\subset\omega_3\subset\cdots \] we denote by \(((x^{p^r}))_{x\in\omega_r}\) the closed ideal of \(k[[\omega]]\) generated by the elements \(x^{p^r}\), where r runs over \(\mathbb{N}\) and x runs over \(\omega_r\). We denote by \({}_r\omega\) the filtered linearly compact vector space such that \[ {}_r\omega_i = \omega_i \ \text{ if } i<r \qquad\text{and}\qquad {}_r\omega_i = \omega \ \text{ if } i\geqslant r. \]

blocks.title: Théorème (Dieudonné-Cartier)
blocks.html:
Theorem (Dieudonné-Cartier)

Let \(H\to G\) be a monomorphism of infinitesimal formal groups over a perfect field k of characteristic \(p>0\). Let B be the affine algebra of the homogeneous space \(H\backslash G\) and suppose that one of the two following conditions is satisfied:

  1. (i)

    B is of finite height (this occurs in particular if G is of finite height).

  2. (ii)

    B is a complete noetherian local ring.

Then B is isomorphic to the completed tensor product of a finite family of truncated formal power series algebras.
The proof of this theorem occupies paragraphs 5.2.1 to 5.2.5.

Let I be the maximal ideal of B and set \(\omega=I/\overline{I^2}\). Denote by \(I_r\) the closed vector subspace of I formed of the x such that \(x^{p^r}=0\), and by \(\omega_r\) the canonical image of \(I_r\) in \(\omega\). We shall see that B is isomorphic to \(k[[\omega]]/((x^{p^r}))_{x\in\omega_r}\) whenever there exists a section \(\sigma : \omega\to I\) of the canonical projection of I onto \(I/\overline{I^2}\) such that \(\sigma(\omega_r)\subset I_r\) for every r: such a section indeed extends to a continuous homomorphism \(t : k[[\omega]]\to B\), which factors through \(B' = k[[\omega]]/((x^{p^r}))_{x\in\omega_r}\). We prove from 5.2.2 to 5.2.5 that the homomorphism \(s : B'\to B\) induced by t is bijective.

What precedes applies in particular when the filtration of \(\omega\) is stationary, that is to say when there exists an integer \(n_0\) such that \(\omega_n=\omega_{n_0}\) for \(n_0\leqslant n<+\infty\). In that case, indeed, one can construct \(\sigma\) first on \(\omega_1\), then on some complement \(\omega'_2\) of \(\omega_1\) in \(\omega_2\), then on some complement \(\omega'_3\) of \(\omega_2\) in \(\omega_3\ldots\), finally on some complement \(\omega'_\infty\) of \(\omega_{n_0}\) in \(\omega\). It is clear that one thus obtains an isomorphism from \(k[[\omega]]/((x^{p^r}))_{x\in\omega_r}\) onto the completed tensor product of the algebras \(k[[\omega_1]]/((x^p))_{x\in\omega_1}\), \(k[[\omega'_2]]/((x^{p^2}))_{x\in\omega'_2}\), \(\ldots\), \(k[[\omega'_{n_0}]]/((x^{p^{n_0}}))_{x\in\omega'_{n_0}}\), \(k[\omega'_\infty]\).

The filtration of \(\omega\) is obviously stationary if \(\omega_r=\omega\) for r large enough (case (i)), or if \(\omega\) is of finite dimension (case (ii)). The statement above therefore implies our theorem.

Suppose first that B is of height \(\leqslant1\), that is to say that \(x^p=0\) if \(x\in I\). It then follows from 5.1.4 that the graded ring \(\mathrm{Gr}_I(B)\) associated to B by the filtration \(I\supset\overline{I^2}\supset\overline{I^3}\cdots\) is equipped with a co-group structure of the category \(\mathrm{GrAl}/k\). If one sets \(C=\prod_{n\geqslant0}\mathrm{Gr}_I(B)_n\), the structural morphism from \(\mathrm{Gr}_I(B)\) to \(\mathrm{Gr}_I(B)\widehat{\otimes}_k\mathrm{Gr}_I(B)\) induces a diagonal morphism \(\Delta_C : C\to C\widehat{\otimes}_kC\) which makes C the affine algebra of a formal group K over k. It is clear that we have \(\omega_{K/k}=I/\overline{I^2}\) and that K is radicial of height \(\leqslant1\). By 4.4, the identity map of \(\omega_{K/k}\) therefore induces an isomorphism from \(k[[\omega_{K/k}]]/((x^p))_{x\in\omega_{K/k}}\) onto C. This implies in particular that the map s of 5.2.1 induces an isomorphism on the graded rings associated to B' and B when one filters B' and B by the powers of the maximal ideals. Therefore s is an isomorphism (C.A. V, §7, lemma 1).

Suppose now that B is of finite height \(\leqslant r\): let \(\pi\) be the isomorphism \(x\mapsto x^p\) from k onto k. The linear map from \(B\widehat{\otimes}_\pi k\) to B which sends \(b\widehat{\otimes}_\pi x\) to \(b^px = (bx^{\frac1p})^p\) has a closed image which is none other than the closed subalgebra \(B^p = \{b^p\mid b\in B\}\) of B. By proposition 5.1, if one sets \(J=B^p\cap I = A\cap I_A\), \(B^p\) is the affine algebra of the left quotient of G by a subgroup H with affine algebra \(A/AJ\). As A is topologically free over B (1.4), B is a direct factor of the pseudocompact B-module A (1.3.2), so that one can identify \(B = B/BJ\) with a closed subalgebra of \(A/AJ\).

By proposition 5.1, \(B_1\) is the affine algebra of a homogeneous space of H and is of height \(\leqslant1\). By 5.2.2, the homomorphism s of 5.2.1 therefore induces an isomorphism from \(B'_1\) onto \(B_1\). If we denote by \(I'\) the maximal ideal of \(B'\), by \(B'^p\) the subalgebra \(\{x^p\mid x\in B'\}\) of \(B'\) and by \(J'\) the maximal ideal of \(B'^p\), this means that the homomorphism \(s : B'\to B\) of 5.2.1 induces an isomorphism from \(B'/B'J'\) onto \(B/BJ\). Now, as A is topologically free over B and over \(B^p\) (5.1 and 1.4), B is topologically free over \(B^p\) (1.3.3); by 0.3.4, s is therefore an isomorphism if the homomorphism \(s^p : B'^p\to B^p\) induced by s is an isomorphism. This is proved in 5.2.4 below.

For every linearly compact vector space V over k, we denote by \({}_\pi V\) the space \(V\widehat{\otimes}_\pi k\) deduced from V by the extension \(x\mapsto x^p\) of the field of scalars. We then have a commutative exact diagram \[ \xymatrix{ 0 \ar[r] & {{}_\pi\overline{I^2}} \ar[r]^-{\alpha} \ar[d]_-u & {{}_\pi I} \ar[r]^-{\beta} \ar[d]_-v & {{}_\pi\omega} \ar[r] \ar[d]^-w & 0 \\ 0 \ar[r] & \overline{J^2} \ar[r]_-{\gamma} & J \ar[r]_-{\delta} & \overline{\omega} \ar[r] & 0 } \] where we have set \(\overline{\omega}=J/\overline{J^2}\) and where the maps u, v, w are induced by the linear map \(x\widehat{\otimes}a \mapsto x^pa\) from \({}_\pi B\) to \(B^p\). If one sets \(J_n=\{x\in J\mid x^{p^n}=0\}\) and \(\overline{\omega}_n=\delta(I_n)\), it is clear that we have \(J_n=v({}_\pi I_{n+1})\) and \(\overline{\omega}_n=w(\omega_{n+1})\). Moreover, as u and v are surjections, w is a surjection and has for kernel the image \(\omega_0\) of the kernel of v. The section \({}_\pi\sigma : {}_\pi\omega \to {}_\pi I\) induced by the section \(\sigma\) of 5.2.1 therefore defines, by passage to the quotient, a section \(\tau : \overline{\omega}\to J\) which is compatible with the filtrations of J and \(\overline{\omega}\). By induction hypothesis, this section induces an isomorphism \(s'\) from \(B'' = k[[\overline{\omega}]]/((x^{p^r}))_{x\in{}_r\overline{\omega}}\) onto \(B^p\). As \(B''\) is manifestly identified with \(B'^p\) and \(s'\) with \(s^p\), our theorem is therefore proved when B is of finite height.

It remains to consider the case where B is of infinite height, and where the canonical projection of I onto \(\omega\) possesses a section compatible with the filtrations of \(\omega\) and I. It suffices to show that for every r, the map \(s_r : B'_r\to B_r\) induced by s is invertible. Now B is a direct factor of the pseudocompact B-module A (1.3.2). If one sets \(L=((x^{p^r}))_{x\in\mathfrak{r}(B)}\), one can therefore identify \(B/BL\) with a closed subalgebra of \(A/AL\); proposition 5.1 shows that \(A/AL\) is the affine algebra of a formal subgroup \(G_r\) of G and that \(B/BL\) is the affine algebra of a homogeneous space of \(G_r\).

Set \(M=I/BL\); the canonical projection of B onto \(B_r\) obviously induces an isomorphism from \(\omega=I/\overline{I^2}\) onto \(M/\overline{M^2}\), which allows us to identify these two spaces. Let then \(\omega_n^r\) be the image in \(\omega\) of the set \(M_n=\{x\in M\mid x^{p^n}=0\}\); let \(I_n^r\) be the inverse image of \(M_n\) in I. The exact sequence \[ 0 \longrightarrow I_n^r\cap\overline{I^2} \longrightarrow I_n^r \longrightarrow \omega_n^r \longrightarrow 0 \] and the equality \(I_n = \bigcap_r I_n^r\) show, by passage to the projective limit, that \(\omega_n\) is the intersection of the \(\omega_n^r\) (\(\mathrm{AB5}^*\), 0.2). Now, as theorem 5.2 is already proved for the algebras of finite height, hence for the \(B_r\), we have \(\omega_n^r=\omega_n^{r+1}\) if \(n<r\) and \(\omega_n^r=\omega\) if \(n\geqslant r\). It follows that we have \(\omega_n^r=\omega_n\) if \(n<r\) and \(\omega_n^r=\omega\) if \(n\geqslant r\).

In other words, the vector space \(\omega\) filtered by the subspaces \(\omega_n^r\) is none other than \({}_r\omega\) (5.2). A fortiori, the map \(\sigma^r\) composed of \(\sigma : \omega\to I\) and the canonical projection of I onto M is compatible with the filtrations \((\omega_n^r)\) and \((M_n)\) of \(\omega\) and M. As \(k[[{}_r\omega]]/((x^{p^n}))_{x\in{}_r\omega_n}\) is none other than \(B'_r\) and \(s_r : B'_r\to B_r\) is the homomorphism induced by \(\sigma^r\), 5.2.4 shows that \(s_r\) is an isomorphism, qed
5.3. Remarks.

Call stationary any profinite algebra over k which is the completed tensor product of a family of truncated formal power series algebras. If G is an infinitesimal formal group over k and B the affine algebra of a homogeneous space of G, it follows from theorem 5.2 that the algebra \(B/((x^{p^r}))_{x\in\mathfrak{r}(B)}\) is stationary for every integer \(r\in\mathbb{N}\). This implies in particular that B is a projective limit of stationary algebras.

I do not know whether, with the notation of 5.2.1, one can choose G and B in such a way that there exists no section \(\sigma : \omega\to I\) compatible with the filtrations. One will note, however, that \(\omega\) can be any linearly compact vector space filtered by an increasing sequence of closed subspaces. If \(\omega_1\subset\omega_2\subset\cdots\subset\omega\) is such a filtered space, one can indeed define in the algebra \(A=k[[\omega]]/((x^{p^r}))_{x\in\omega_r}\) a diagonal morphism \(\Delta_A : A\to A\widehat{\otimes}_kA\) satisfying conditions (i), (ii), (iii) of 2.1; it suffices to set \(\Delta_Ay = y\widehat{\otimes}1+1\widehat{\otimes}y\) when y is the image in A of an element of \(\omega\).

blocks.title: Corollaire
blocks.html:
Corollary 5.4

Let G be an algebraic group over a perfect field k of characteristic \(p>0\), H an algebraic subgroup of G, e the image of the identity element of G in \(H\backslash G\) and A the local algebra of \(H\backslash G\) at e. Then \(\widehat{A}\) is isomorphic to an algebra of the form \[ k[[X_1,\ldots,X_r,\ldots X_s]]/(X_1^{p^{n_1}},\ldots,X_r^{p^{n_r}}). \]
This follows from 5.2 (ii) and 1.3.4.
